source.transcript:
Header: 2027-11-08 17:00 UTC | Duration: 58 mins | Attendees: Maya, Ethan, Priya, Liam, Rosa, ~150 company staff

[00:00] Maya Chen: okay good afternoon everyone, thanks for joining the Orchestra Command brief, we're gonna run 55, 60 minutes, quick updates and then Q&A — a lot to cover so i'll keep it tight.
[00:17] Maya Chen: high level — runway looks fine, revenue growth is slightly above plan, but cost per token is where we need to double down. we have to keep our position as the predictable serving layer.
[00:34] Ethan Park: yeah, on the infra side we're seeing three levers: better kernel selection per arch, more aggressive prefix caching, and smarter routing to cheaper variants. those together are what we told customers in the benchmarks but we need operational telemetry.
[00:50] Priya Rao: product goal is to ship two experiments in the next 30 days: one, an Optimize UI to surface batching suggestions for top customers; two, a Dedicated onboarding flow that reduces time-to-config from days to hours.
[01:08] Liam O'Connor: engineering's focused on the auto-batching backend and a refactor of the kv cache management; note that the new quantization path introduced some flakiness in longer sequences — investigating.
[01:25] Staff (murmur): (crosstalk)
[01:30] Maya Chen: quick reminder — customers keep asking about Private/VPC on prem timelines and SOC2 rescope for 2028. we need a clear ops story for the next RFP.
[01:45] Rosa Martinez: hiring update — we're still under plan for infra senior ICs, pipeline is warming but time-to-offer is stretched. we will prioritize top-of-funnel ops and kernel folks.
[02:02] Maya Chen: ok let's walk through the numbers and then do a short demo from product. Ethan, can you pull the perf slide?
[02:12] Ethan Park: sure, on slide 3 (link: internal deck) — bucketed latency distribution shows tail latency improved by 12% in us-west with kernel patch v4, cost per 1k tokens down 7% in the test cohort. 
[02:30] Ethan Park: caveat — test cohort was largely short-sequence chat, for longer sequence LLMs the quant path adds variance.
[02:45] Priya Rao: from product-facing side, sales has asked for a clearer articulation: Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private. we need a one-pager that includes costs, latency ranges, and compliance tradeoffs.
[03:03] Maya Chen: agreed. Priya, coordinate with AE team, put together a matrix and include SSO/KMS/HSM notes by next Tuesday.
[03:18] Priya Rao: noted.
[03:22] Audience question (Attendee 1): how are we planning to surface model regressions to customers?
[03:30] Liam O'Connor: good question — plan is twofold: per-route evals with synthetic prompts plus alerting for metric drift. Redwood Console will show the eval run history and link to the prompt sets.
[03:50] Maya Chen: want to emphasize we should not overpromise exact deterministic outputs; we offer instrumented confidence and fallbacks. remind sales that fallback policies exist.
[04:06] Ethan Park: on fallbacks, routing will prefer latency SLO compatibility then cost buckets; if an edge region is out we'll route to a compatible model variant, auto-quantized if needed. there are some open UX bits.
[04:26] Maya Chen: okay quick ops risk overview — what keeps you up at night, Ethan?
[04:33] Ethan Park: supply-side: GPU churn and driver regressions; demand-side: bursty PLG usage patterns. we need better autoscaling smoothing and capacity commitments for Dedicated clients.
[04:48] Priya Rao: also want to flag the regen onboarding friction for customers who want private air-gapped deployments; there's legal and networking gating.
[05:02] Rosa Martinez: folks — hiring will be temporarily re-prioritized towards infra SRE and security roles; interview loops will be tightened.
[05:15] Maya Chen: on GTM, we've seen pockets of expansion with two mid-market customers converting from hosted to Dedicated after pilot. that validates the Reserved capacity motion.
[05:29] Maya Chen: finance asked for visibility on unit economics; Product and Eng should present a breakdown next week.
[05:37] Priya Rao: we'll do a token-cost heatmap and annotate where caching and batching save the most.
[05:47] Audience reaction: (applause)
[05:55] Q&A start — cross-functional: people ops, engineering mangers asking specifics.
[06:10] Attendee (Engineering Manager): can we get a deep dive on the kernel selection and which models benefit most?
[06:18] Ethan Park: yes we'll schedule a technical deep-dive; short answer: decoder-only open models show biggest perf gains with the new fused attention kernels, encoder-decoder models less so.
[06:33] Attendee: any timeline on per-tenant KMS/HSM support?
[06:39] Liam O'Connor: MVP for KMS integration is targeted end of Q1 2028; HSM longer — requires certs, legal and partner work.
[06:52] Maya Chen: let's move to the Product demo, Priya?
[07:00] Priya Rao: demo note: quick walk-through of Optimize suggestions UI (link: demo recording). shows a list of routes where batching could save 18–40% and recommended cache policies.
[07:18] Priya Rao: real-time scoring ties to our evals, and we have a beta toggle for automatic application of suggested cache policies for Dedicated accounts.
[07:32] Audience (low-volume): uh-huh, nice.
[07:35] Maya Chen: that beta toggle — need guardrails and rollback. Liam, engineering to own rollback hooks.
[07:43] Liam O'Connor: confirmed. we'll ship with fallback disabled by default and an opt-in flag for accounts.
[07:52] Attendee (Sales Rep): any change to pricing model for Dedicated?
[07:57] Priya Rao: pricing conversation is happening; short-term updates for new Reserved customers will be more nuanced: discounts tied to committed throughput, plus an add-on for extended support.
[08:12] Maya Chen: remember positioning: we sell predictability more than cheapest per-token.
[08:20] Rosa Martinez: people update: upcoming all-hands survey going live Friday, please participate; we're also rolling a new parental leave policy next quarter.
[08:32] Maya Chen: thanks Rosa. ok lightning round — three wins in the last 30 days? Ethan?
[08:40] Ethan Park: kernel v4 reduced tail latency for chat by 12% (already said), internal benchmarks for auto-batching look promising, and we closed the contract with a european fintech client for Dedicated.
[08:56] Priya Rao: launched a templates repo for prompt evals and customers are starting to onboard their eval suites.
[09:06] Rosa Martinez: hired two senior SREs and promoted an engineering manager into infra lead.
[09:15] Maya Chen: alright risks and asks — product and eng need three things: 1) clearer OKR mapping to revenue; 2) faster experimental cadence for Optimize; 3) a prioritized list of security requirements for the RFP pipeline.
[09:34] Priya Rao: we'll map OKRs to the revenue plays and share next Tuesday.
[09:40] Liam O'Connor: engineering will schedule the kernel deep-dive and produce an ops-runbook for Dedicated migration.
[09:50] Audience question (Attendee 2): any update on international regions? latency improvements or new zones?
[09:56] Ethan Park: exploring a eu-central region partner placement Q1 2028; working with cloud partners on contracts.
[10:07] Maya Chen: final notes — everyone, please review the priorities doc after this call. remember: execution and risk mitigation. we'll track open items in the orchestration board.
[10:22] Maya Chen: ok, thanks all. next all-hands will be a shorter rhythm check in four weeks.
[10:31] (meeting ends)

Recap / Actions (auto):
- Maya to publish Q4 priorities doc by 2027-11-12
- Ethan to run infra blast-radius tabletop 2027-11-15
- Priya to deliver Hosted/Dedicated/Private one-pager 2027-11-12

Notes: references to slides and demo recordings were shared in chat (internal links). Transcription contains minor errors around kernel names and acronyms (e.g., kv cache sometimes transcribed as 'k-v cash').

source.transcript:
Meeting header: 2027-03-08 17:00 UTC | Duration: ~62 minutes | Location: All-hands town virtual | Attendees: company-wide

Summary: (auto) Leadership shared the quarterly baton for roadmap ownership, clarified a two-phase release strategy for key features, and committed to hiring + infra experiments. Questions surfaced re: Dedicated capacity and SSO audits.

[00:00] Ava Chen: okay hey everyone thanks for joining on time, um we're going to kick off, we've got a packed agenda so we'll try to keep things tight, I want to start with a quick status on the roadmap baton handoff.

[00:12] Diego Martinez: yeah thanks Ava, real quick one, we've been seeing the usual seasonal uptick in request volumes and some tail latency on longer sequences, so the infra team has been focusing on KV cache warm-up and batching tweaks, a few of those kernels we rolled last month look promising but not yet grokked into prod traffic patterns.

[00:36] Sonia Patel: to add - product will split the Q2 features into "Phase A" for low-risk launches and "Phase B" for high-impact experiments, the idea is to reduce blast radius when we turn on model variants or quantized builds. We'll share the matrix after this meeting.

[00:50] Marcus Lee: hiring note - our revised plan slows down lateral hires for infra while prioritising SRE and core serving runtime engineers; we need capacity to own the Dedicated pools and to shorten our SLA remediation windows. We're lining up three reqs for the next two weeks.

[01:06] Priya Nair: quick HR note - compensation calibration is delayed by one week, apologies, but we will keep merit increases separate from role rebanding. Also launching a peer recognition pilot next month.

[01:22] Ava Chen: thanks. Quick framing: we are balancing two paths - growth (faster onboarding, self-serve) and enterprise readiness (Dedicated, Private). We can't be everything at once so the baton here is to stabilize the serving backbone so growth doesn't break for our paying customers.

[01:40] Speaker 7: (murmur) question about data residency - are we still prioritizing EU region expansion in Q2?

[01:46] Diego Martinez: yes EU expansion remains a priority, but the gating item is certs and audit readiness; we'll only add capacity once the compliance checklist is clear. The security team is working on audit logs and KMS integrations.

[02:05] Sonia Patel: on rollouts - when we flip Phase A features we'll include canary keys and a default rollback window. Phase B will require an explicit ops-on-call during initial hours. This should help with canine... (laughs) canary, sorry.

[02:22] [laughter]

[02:30] Ava Chen: that's intentional, we want to be conservative. Okay let's go into a few specifics - Diego, can you explain the Dedicated backlog and why some customer pilots are waiting?

[02:40] Diego Martinez: sure - backlog is mostly capacity allocation plus a small amount of automation work. Dedicated requires predictable GPU pools; some of the automation for multi-tenant scheduling is behind. We also have telemetry gaps for tail-latency on long context chats. I'm planning a focused sprint to close the telemetry + scheduler tasks in three weeks.

[03:02] Marcus Lee: and from hiring perspective we need those SREs so we can shift operational burden off the product teams. Right now on-call is flat-out for some pods.

[03:14] Speaker 4 (engineer): hi um small thing - could we get more clarity on on-call rotations? some teams are running 24/7 with a single escalation path and it's tough.

[03:26] Priya Nair: yes, we're doing a new rotation policy pilot - it shortens rotations and creates a secondary responder to reduce context-switching. We'll pilot with infra and platform teams.

[03:35] Ava Chen: great. Next major item - competitive posture. We're getting questions about how we differentiate from larger providers. Diego?

[03:44] Diego Martinez: short answer: we trade on predictable economics and developer ergonomics. Technically, we're focused on optimized kernels, continuous batching and model routing - that combo gives customers consistent per-token costs. That said, messaging needs work; Sonia will own a crisp positioning doc.

[04:02] Sonia Patel: yep we'll pull together a one-pager that calls out the unified runtime, Dedicated guarantees, and our quality/regression tooling. We'll avoid buzzword-itis but highlight unit economics.

[04:15] [00:04:20] Speaker 9: question about SOC2 and SAML - are we on track?

[04:24] Ava Chen: security update - we have a planned SOC2 audit timeline and the crank for SSO/SAML and RBAC is moving. Security will publish a Q2 compliance playbook next week.

[04:38] Diego Martinez: also note that Private deployments have extra integration work - HSM and customer KMS varies wildly, so expect longer timelines for on-prem.

[04:50] Ava Chen: okay, we've got a short demo from Marcus on the rollout control plane. Marcus?

[04:55] Marcus Lee: demo time. So the release control plane gives PMs a simple interface to set canary percentages, pin model variants, and enforce fallbacks. There's also a triage view for incidents. I'm going to share the recording and the repo link to the demo app. Forgive some rough edges, it's early.

[05:24] [demo plays - indistinct audio; some folks typing] 

[06:10] Speaker 6: (inaudible) does the control plane support cross-region fallbacks?

[06:18] Marcus Lee: yes, it's policy-driven - you can set region preference, latency thresholds and a failover chain. We're testing the automatic fallback to cheaper models during high load.

[06:33] Sonia Patel: note that product will require a regression test set for each rollout, those tests will be tied to the quality monitoring dashboard so we can get alerts if a model change drifts.

[06:48] Ava Chen: good. Let's jump into Q&A - please use the raise-hand or chat.

[06:55] [08:00] Chat: request - can we get a better docs page for Dedicated on capacity planning?

[08:18] Diego Martinez: yes, docs team is working with infra to publish capacity planning guidance - we'll include sample pooling configs and recommended batch sizes.

[08:30] Speaker 11: (voice) what's the plan for reducing inference cost per 1M tokens? customers keep asking.

[08:40] Diego Martinez: three levers - smarter batching, KV-prefix caching, and recommending quantized variants where acceptable. Redwood Optimize will start surfacing quantization tradeoffs per workload.

[09:02] Sonia Patel: and product will publish a cost playbook for customers that explains tradeoffs and default policies.

[09:15] [10:00] Speaker 3: concern - we've had a few incidents where the rollback didn't complete cleanly and left degraded latencies for 15 minutes. Any mitigation?

[10:20] Marcus Lee: we're adding a safety circuit in the control plane: an automated verification step post-rollback; if metrics don't converge within X minutes it'll trigger a second fallback and paging.

[10:36] Ava Chen: good. A few operational bullets - one, we'll pause any new large-scale marketing spikes until after the rollouts are stable; two, sales: please don't sign large Dedicated pilots that require bespoke infra until the automation pieces are in.

[10:55] [11:12] Speaker 2: question from support - how are we improving postmortems?

[11:20] Priya Nair: postmortem playbook is being updated with timelines and required artifacts. We'll also roll out a blameless template and a follow-up action tracker.

[11:35] Ava Chen: closing section - leadership asks teams to focus on three things: one, reliability; two, measured rollouts; three, hiring where it removes a critical bottleneck. We'll revisit in two weeks with status updates.

[11:52] [12:00] Q&A continues with rapid-fire questions - some are about travel policy, some about the new peer program, a few about internal tooling access.

[12:18] Diego Martinez: quick note - infra will run a bench test this weekend simulating 100k concurrent streams; results will go to the platform channel. Expect some noisy alerts while we run it.

[12:30] Sonia Patel: product ops will schedule a small cohort pilot for Phase A features starting 2027-03-22. We'll invite EMs and a fam of early customers.

[12:44] Ava Chen: thank you all - last thing: I'm proud of the work everyone is doing, but we need clear tradeoffs. We'll signpost decisions earlier to reduce rework. Ok we'll wrap, please check the follow-ups in the #all-hands-notes doc and the action tracker.

[12:58] Speaker 8: thanks Ava, thanks everyone.

[13:04] Meeting end.

Notes: audio clarity varied across campus - a few segments have low signal. References: demo repo link (shared in chat), draft rollout matrix (to be uploaded).

next_steps: Priya to update runbook with communication tree and publish by 2026-12-10
Mateo to run synthetic throughput test against canary model at 0600 PST on 2026-12-09
Ellen to attach SOC2 appendix and SSO checklist to the launch packet by EOD 2026-12-08
Ava to draft customer-facing downtime bulletin and share for review 2026-12-09
action_items: Priya Nair: consolidate pager rotation + escalation matrix; due 2026-12-10
Mateo Ruiz: smoke test script for KV cache warm-up and cold-start latency; due 2026-12-09

source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-12-08
Start: 10:00 UTC | Duration: ~78 minutes
Attendees: Ava, Mateo, Rajat, Maya, Priya, Ben, Sophie, Connor, Ellen, Diego

Summary:
Quick launch communication drill focused on who speaks to customers during degradations, last-mile runbook edits, and triage of three late-stage risks: kernel fallback latency spike, KV warming regression, and outage communications cadence. Ran through a simulated degraded scenario and validated handoffs between SEs, release, and ops.

Topics covered:
- Communication tree and customer bulleting cadence
- Live-run simulation results for canary 2
- Risk #1: kernel fallback causing 2x tail latency at 95th pct
- Risk #2: cold-start KV cache warming causing token-cost spike
- Risk #3: SSO + SAML flakiness on private deployments during region failover
- Rollback vs degrade decisions and automated fallback triggers

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava Chen: okay, let's get started. thanks everyone for joining the drill. the goal is to exercise the comms path and late stage triage so we all know who says what if we see a tail-latency incident during launch. priya, want to kick with the comms tree?
[00:18] Priya Nair: yeah, sure. so top-level, we have a Tier 0 pager for P0s. on the customer side we notify account owners within 5 minutes, public status page after 15, targeted emails for impacted customers after 30 minutes, and then a follow-up within 24 hours. page rotation is the same as normal oncall schedule but we add an extra release lead for the first 72 hours.
[00:38] Ben Howard: small note — add a single designated AE point to avoid multiple outreach. we had conflicting messages last cycle, customers were confused.
[00:46] Ava Chen: agreed, that was ugly. let's say Connor is the AE liaison for this launch — he'll clear external wording with me and Priya before it goes out.
[00:56] Connor Patel: i'll do that, happy to.
[00:58] Mateo Ruiz: ok, simulated failure we ran earlier — canary 2 saw a fallback to the quantized kernel at about 11:43 PST, and we observed a trailing tail from 200 ms to ~450 ms p95 but p99 spiked to around 1.2s on longer contexts. i have logs, but quick primer: kernel selection logic overreacted to transient memory pressure.
[01:24] Rajat Singh: memory pressure as in OOM throttles or just scheduler backpressure?
[01:28] Mateo Ruiz: scheduler backpressure plus a small gc on the runtime that freed addresses slower, and the fallback path uses a different attention kernel that's single-threaded for short batches.
[01:40] Maya Torres: that rings a bell — we merged the kernel fallback last sprint to address a rare oom. the test harness didn't exercise long context tails.
[01:48] Ellen Zhao: from security — i want to call out that during the fallback we also saw an extra auth check failing for SAML assertions on private nodes, likely a timing edge. not necessarily exploitable but makes customer-facing messaging tricky.
[02:02] Diego Marquez: that's the private cluster peering case we worried about; region failover added a slight clock skew and the SAML validation window is short.
[02:12] Ava Chen: okay, so three buckets: performance tail from kernel fallback, KV-cache cold-start cost increase, and SAML timing issues. let's walk the simulated script. priya you will role-play comms?
[02:22] Priya Nair: yes. simulated scenario: canary latency degradation at T0, engineering determines model fallback is happening, decide whether to degrade to smaller model or roll back the kernel change. we need to exercise decision trees.
[02:34] Ben Howard: i triggered the sim at 11:43 in the test cluster. Mateo, you have logs?
[02:40] Mateo Ruiz: sending link now — scoreboard.log and kernel-monitor.csv (link: internal:/canary2/sim-2026-12-08). note the spike aligns with concurrent large-context requests.
[02:54] [crosstalk, someone coughs] 
[03:00] Sophie Li: quick question: what are our SLO thresholds that trip an automated rollback vs manual intervention? do we have a firm number?
[03:07] Rajat Singh: current rollout config uses a p95 latency threshold of 300ms and a p99 threshold of 900ms for automated rollback on canary. but those were tuned for smaller contexts. for long contexts the p95 baseline is already higher.
[03:22] Mateo Ruiz: right — and the automatic policy didn't account for sequence-length distribution shift. the canary had an unusual batch of long prompts coming through which skewed metrics.
[03:30] Ava Chen: so we've got a false positive risk. priya, what's the decision tree if automatic rollback fires erroneously?
[03:38] Priya Nair: fallback: if auto rollback triggers, oncall gets paged and release lead decides within 10 minutes to confirm rollback. we also have a temp-degrade option in the routing policy to shift some percent of traffic to a smaller model instead of rolling back. that's less disruptive to releases but may change cost and output.
[03:52] Ben Howard: we practiced the temp-degrade yesterday; it took about 6 minutes to shift policy and 18 minutes for key caches to warm, so customers experienced a short period of elevated cost.
[04:04] Ava Chen: note for comms: we need to call out likely impact — short spike in latency, potential higher token costs for some customers, and a follow-up with more detailed post-mortem. keep the message short and avoid technical blame.
[04:18] Ellen Zhao: and include that SSO flakiness was observed but not confirmed as exploit. re security, we'll write an appendix explaining mitigations and the timeline for SAML patching.
[04:30] Diego Marquez: for private deployments, customers may need to restart the auth adapter if the clock skew hits them. include the restart instructions in the runbook.
[04:40] Priya Nair: action: priya will add that instruction to runbook; ben will write the one-liner for the customer bulletin.
[04:48] [keyboard tap noises] 
[05:00] Mateo Ruiz: returning to the KV cache warming issue — we saw a token-cost spike when cold nodes served requests; cost per token went up because requests fell back to longer compute path. the autoscaler later spun up, but the initial 12 minutes matter.
[05:18] Rajat Singh: can we warm caches faster? maybe prefetch the top prefixes for high-traffic tenants during rollout?
[05:24] Mateo Ruiz: yes, we can push a quick warm-up hook in the rollout plan to prepopulate KV for the top 50 tenants. script exists but needs integration with the release pipeline.
[05:35] Maya Torres: we should also throttle the canary traffic by certificate in the LB and make sure the initial burst doesn't trigger autoscaler oscillations.
[05:44] Priya Nair: ok prioritization: 1) add canary pre-warm hook, 2) adjust automatic SLO thresholds to be sequence-length-aware, 3) update comms templates to avoid confusing technical detail.
[05:56] Ava Chen: timeline on those?
[06:00] Mateo Ruiz: pre-warm hook — i can do a PR today and request merge tomorrow; integration might take until friday.
[06:06] Rajat Singh: SLO tuning — i'll follow up with product for sequence-length signals and we can deploy a metric adjustment by 2026-12-11.
[06:14] Ellen Zhao: i'll produce the security appendix tonight.
[06:18] Priya Nair: comms templates — i'll draft versions for P0 and P1 with one-line, three-line and full postmortem slots by EOD tomorrow.
[06:26] [20 minute simulation jump — team runs through scenario offline] 
[26:40] Ava Chen: back on the call. the simulation highlighted a confusing handoff between SE and release lead. specifically, the AE got customer questions before the release lead had a decision. we should formalize the pre-approval phrase.
[26:56] Diego Marquez: propose a canonical sentence: "We are investigating a limited degradation affecting generation latency for long-context requests; engineering is actively assessing and we will provide an update within 30 minutes." short, non-technical.
[27:12] Ava Chen: looks good. Mateo, if engineering confirms rollback, what's the expected customer-visible delta and how quickly can you revert?
[27:20] Mateo Ruiz: a rollback to previous kernel variant typically takes 4-7 minutes to complete on the control plane and another 8-12 for caches to stabilize under normal load. for long contexts, p99 may still be elevated until caches are fully warmed.
[27:34] Rajat Singh: important callout — a rollback also means we lose the small memory-safety gain from the new fallback; that risk is low for now but note it in the decision log.
[27:44] Ben Howard: who owns the decision log entry?
[27:46] Priya Nair: release lead — priya will own the decision log and commit it to the launch ops channel.
[27:52] Ava Chen: good. now let's role-play the AE message to customers. priya, can you read the short and the extended?
[28:00] Priya Nair: short: "We are investigating a limited degradation affecting generation latency for long-context requests; engineering is actively assessing and we will provide an update within 30 minutes." extended: "We observed increased latency for longer-context requests tied to a kernel fallback path exercised on canary nodes during rollout. We have mitigations in flight and may roll back the recent kernel change if the regression persists. Impact is localized to longer contexts and a subset of tenants; we'll provide timelines as they become available and a postmortem within 24 hours."
[28:34] Ava Chen: that's good. Diego — add a line for customers on dedicated/private setups about restarting the auth adapter if they see SAML errors.
[28:44] Diego Marquez: done. priya will include in the template.
[28:48] [brief silence] 
[29:00] Sophie Li: i want to flag downstream product expectations — some customers embed long prompts as single messages; this launch has a higher proportion of those users. should we ramp slower for those accounts?
[29:12] Ava Chen: yes, we can opt those accounts out of fast ramp and move them through a manual staging cadence. we need to identify top 30 accounts and mark them in the rollout dashboard.
[29:24] Mateo Ruiz: also reminder that the fallback kernel is single-threaded for small batches; for customers with bursty single requests it will hurt more. we can set per-account policy to avoid fallback for high-value tenants.
[29:36] Rajat Singh: let's not make the per-account policy too heavy — prefer a small set of opt-outs.
[29:42] Priya Nair: action: priya + sophie to create the opt-out list and strategy; due 2026-12-09.
[29:50] [keyboard clicking] 
[30:00] Ben Howard: incident comms cadence rehearsal — who reads from the playbook?
[30:04] Priya Nair: i'll read.
[30:06] Ben Howard: ok, then we simulate a follow up 30 minutes later where engineering confirms rollback. priya, craft the 30-minute update.

Quick launch communication drill focused on who speaks to customers during degradations, last-mile runbook edits, and triage of three late-stage risks: kernel fallback latency spike, KV warming regression, and outage communications cadence. Ran through a simulated degraded scenario and validated handoffs between SEs, release, and ops.

Topics covered:
- Communication tree and customer bulleting cadence
- Live-run simulation results for canary 2
- Risk #1: kernel fallback causing 2x tail latency at 95th pct
- Risk #2: cold-start KV cache warming causing token-cost spike
- Risk #3: SSO + SAML flakiness on private deployments during region failover
- Rollback vs degrade decisions and automated fallback triggers

[03:22] Mateo Ruiz: right — and the automatic policy didn't account for sequence-length distribution shift. the canary had an unusual batch of long prompts coming through which skewed metrics.
[03:30] Ava Chen: so we've got a false positive risk. priya, what's the decision tree if automatic rollback fires erroneously?
[03:38] Priya Nair: fallback: if auto rollback triggers, oncall gets paged and release lead decides within 10 minutes to confirm rollback. we also have a temp-degrade option in the routing policy to shift some percent of traffic to a smaller model instead of rolling back. that's less disruptive to releases but may change cost and output.
[03:52] Ben Howard: we practiced the temp-degrade yesterday; it took about 6 minutes to shift policy and 18 minutes for key caches to warm, so customers experienced a short period of elevated cost.
[04:04] Ava Chen: note for comms: we need to call out likely impact — short spike in latency, potential higher token costs for some customers, and a follow-up with more detailed post-mortem. keep the message short and avoid technical blame.
[04:18] Ellen Zhao: and include that SSO flakiness was observed but not confirmed as exploit. re security, we'll write an appendix explaining mitigations and the timeline for SAML patching.
[04:30] Diego Marquez: for private deployments, customers may need to restart the auth adapter if the clock skew hits them. include the restart instructions in the runbook.
[04:40] Priya Nair: action: priya will add that instruction to runbook; ben will write the one-liner for the customer bulletin.
[04:48] [keyboard tap noises] 
[05:00] Mateo Ruiz: returning to the KV cache warming issue — we saw a token-cost spike when cold nodes served requests; cost per token went up because requests fell back to longer compute path. the autoscaler later spun up, but the initial 12 minutes matter.
[05:18] Rajat Singh: can we warm caches faster? maybe prefetch the top prefixes for high-traffic tenants during rollout?
[05:24] Mateo Ruiz: yes, we can push a quick warm-up hook in the rollout plan to prepopulate KV for the top 50 tenants. script exists but needs integration with the release pipeline.
[05:35] Maya Torres: we should also throttle the canary traffic by certificate in the LB and make sure the initial burst doesn't trigger autoscaler oscillations.
[05:44] Priya Nair: ok prioritization: 1) add canary pre-warm hook, 2) adjust automatic SLO thresholds to be sequence-length-aware, 3) update comms templates to avoid confusing technical detail.
[05:56] Ava Chen: timeline on those?
[06:00] Mateo Ruiz: pre-warm hook — i can do a PR today and request merge tomorrow; integration might take until friday.
[06:06] Rajat Singh: SLO tuning — i'll follow up with product for sequence-length signals and we can deploy a metric adjustment by 2026-12-11.
[06:14] Ellen Zhao: i'll produce the security appendix tonight.
[06:18] Priya Nair: comms templates — i'll draft versions for P0 and P1 with one-line, three-line and full postmortem slots by EOD tomorrow.
[06:26] [20 minute simulation jump — team runs through scenario offline]

[27:20] Mateo Ruiz: a rollback to previous kernel variant typically takes 4-7 minutes to complete on the control plane and another 8-12 for caches to stabilize under normal load. for long contexts, p99 may still be elevated until caches are fully warmed.
[27:34] Rajat Singh: important callout — a rollback also means we lose the small memory-safety gain from the new fallback; that risk is low for now but note it in the decision log.
[27:44] Ben Howard: who owns the decision log entry?
[27:46] Priya Nair: release lead — priya will own the decision log and commit it to the launch ops channel.
[27:52] Ava Chen: good. now let's role-play the AE message to customers. priya, can you read the short and the extended?
[28:00] Priya Nair: short: "We are investigating a limited degradation affecting generation latency for long-context requests; engineering is actively assessing and we will provide an update within 30 minutes." extended: "We observed increased latency for longer-context requests tied to a kernel fallback path exercised on canary nodes during rollout. source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-10-21 14:00 PT
Duration: ~1h18m
Attendees: Ava, Diego, Priya, Liam, Morgan, Aria, Noah, Sofia, Ethan, Janelle

Auto-generated summary (may be rough): Align on resource corridor and degrade policy for user flows; plan canary and tooling updates.

[00:00] Ava Chen: okay, let's kick off, thanks everyone for being here. The goal for today is pretty pragmatic — we need to land on a corridor definition and a concrete degrade policy we can canary next sprint.

[00:24] Aria Patel: hi all, quick context, the Oct 10 cold-start spike burned through our headroom and it exposed that we don't have a consistent glidepath for traffic degradation, different products did different things. we want uniformity and predictable user impact.

[01:05] Diego Ramos: yeah and to add, some flows went to a cheaper model variant, others started dropping non-critical tooling. it's messy. we need explicit tiers: reserve, nominal, burst, and emergency — and actions tied to those tiers.

[01:42] Priya Singh: when you say "reserve" are we talking X percent of cluster capacity per region or a global pooled buffer? I prefer regional corridors for latency-sensitive routes.

[02:10] Noah Kim: region-first is right for latency, but for some low-PR flows we can fall back to a global pool. we should classify routes by latency-sensitivity.

[02:45] Liam O'Connor: also remember KV cache effects — when a region loses cache warmness you get a multiplicative regen. any corridor math needs to account for warmup debt.

[03:18] Sofia Martinez: note — transcript generator mis-typed kv as "kay vee cash" in the previous doc, but yes, cache warmup is huge. we should include a short burn-in budget in the corridor for that.

[04:00] Ethan Brooks: propose corridors: reserve = 10% of peak p99, nominal = typical, burst lane = +20% transient for 15m windows, emergency = throttle non-essential routes and switch to quantized variants.

[04:38] Priya Singh: +1 to the burst window idea. make it configurable per customer tier (hosted vs dedicated) — dedicated customers might want larger reserved lanes.

[05:10] Morgan Lee: from product ops we need the UX story: what does "degrade" look like to end users? reduced context, lower token cap, or slower streaming? need consistent messaging.

[05:42] Aria Patel: agreed. categorize degradations into soft degrade and hard degrade. soft: reduce freshness (e.g., reduce embedding frequency), lower max tokens, quiet logging. hard: model switch, route rejects, or full rate-limit.

[06:15] Diego Ramos: we should enumerate a prioritized action list for each tier. example: burst -> scale up autoscaler first, then enable caching warmup priority, then soft degrade; emergency -> hard degrade list.

[06:50] Janelle Wu: operationally we need precise triggers. what metric and window defines crossing into burst vs emergency? Is it p95 CPU? queue length? sustained thread-saturation?

[07:24] Liam O'Connor: recommend composite triggers: (1) sustained host CPU>85% for 5m OR (2) request queue length > threshold for 2m AND error rate trending up. combo gives better signal.

[08:02] Priya Singh: and tie it to cost signals for hosted — e.g., if our spend-per-token goes >X for 10m then go to degrade to avoid runaway.

[08:35] Ava Chen: good. now, degrade rule engine — config model: declarative rules per route: priority, allowed degradations ordered list, thresholds. Diego, can you sketch an interface?

[09:02] Diego Ramos: yeah i sketched a JSON-ish example in the doc: route {priority, regionAffinity, degradations:[{type, param, fallback}]}. i'll push that. note the grammar may be fuzzy in notes — call it proto-config.

[09:35] Noah Kim: what's our plan for testing these rules? we can't take prod risk without a canary plan.

[09:50] Janelle Wu: canary = small subset of customers + synthetic traffic ramp. define windows, success criteria (latency, error rate, retention of key behaviour), and rollback triggers.

[10:20] Priya Singh: also propose a "graceful stretch" mode where we temporarily widen burst lanes for a small percentage of traffic to let infra heal. that's different from degrade — it's expansion with limits.

[10:50] Ethan Brooks: for model-switch degrade, we need to ensure deterministic output shape; tool-calling and function support must still work. so switch only to variants that match API contracts.

[11:25] Diego Ramos: note: quantized variant might increase mismatch. add a QA step in canary to validate prompt regressions on a small suite.

[12:00] Morgan Lee: what about telemetry? product wants user-perceivable metrics and a public status message; engineering wants internal signals. we need both in the event page.

[12:28] Liam O'Connor: add per-route degradation annotations in traces and in the request logs so we can correlate user-visible regressions with infra events.

[13:05] Aria Patel: incident review quick takeaways from Oct 10: inconsistent thresholds across teams, no single rule engine, manual rollbacks cost time. This is why we need a unified degrade policy.

[13:45] Ava Chen: ok let's get concrete — define three policy tiers: Tier A (latency-critical), Tier B (standard), Tier C (batch/async). For each tier list: reserve percent, allowed soft-degrades, allowed hard-degrades.

[14:20] Priya Singh: propose Tier A reserve 12% regional, Tier B 8%, Tier C 4% but Tier C can be global pooled.

[14:58] Diego Ramos: implement default actions: Tier A -> attempt autoscale, then soft degrade (reduce tokens, drop non-essential tool calls), Tier B -> prefer soft degrade earlier, Tier C -> aggressive model switch or queueing.

[15:36] Janelle Wu: we'll need playbook snippets for each stage with explicit rollback links and runbook owners. I'll own the runbook drafts.

[16:05] Ethan Brooks: on the tooling side, the degrade-engine should emit events to the timeline service and to a dedicated channel in slack. quick ops visibility reduces MTTR.

[16:40] Priya Singh: about data residency and private deployments — private customers may want to control degrade behavior; we should expose policy as a customer-configurable object with safe defaults.

[17:10] Noah Kim: we need gating for private: safety checks before allowing customer-specified hard-degrade to ensure they can't silently doorbell other tenants.

[17:42] Ava Chen: excellent. now let's talk metrics we will track during canary: p50/p95 latency, error rate, token throughput, downstream user-visible failures, and a prompt-regression score from the eval harness.

[18:15] Diego Ramos: prompt-regression score — we'll run 200 golden prompts and compute a similarity/regression metric pre/post degrade. Threshold for acceptance = less than 2% regression at aggregate.

[18:48] Morgan Lee: we should also track business metrics: revenue per minute of latency, and conversion drop for degraded AX flows, so product owners can assess tradeoffs.

[19:20] Aria Patel: operational timeline suggestion: week 1 design and proto, week 2 prototype and local tests, week 3 internal canary with synthetic traffic, week 4 small-customer canary, then broader rollout.

[19:50] Priya Singh: caveat: kernel-level quantization work is ongoing; we can't rely on quantized model switches until next kernel patch. mark that as a dependency.

[20:12] Liam O'Connor: link the dependency to the kernel release ticket; otherwise the canary must avoid quant-variant options.

[20:40] Diego Ramos: I'll add feature flags to gate soft/hard degrade and we should be able to flip them per region.

[21:05] Janelle Wu: and I will pre-authorize rollback owners for each canary wave. reduce decision latency.

[21:35] Ava Chen: open floor for concerns.

[21:44] Ethan Brooks: small ask — ensure the degrade-engine supports a manual override with reason metadata so we can audit decisions.

[22:05] Priya Singh: yes audit + tie to KMS logs and RBAC. we need compliance-friendly trails.

[22:30] Morgan Lee: product question — are there customer communication templates? we need standard language for status page and email.

[22:55] Aria Patel: I'll draft a short template and attach it to the decision memo.

[23:10] Ava Chen: recap — deliverables: corridor sizing model (Priya), rule-engine proto (Diego), alerts + playbook (Liam), canary schedule (Janelle), decision memo & template (Ava/Aria).

[23:35] Diego Ramos: one more note — consider a "degradation budget" metric that shrinks over time if we keep degrading; incentivizes rapid remediation.

[24:00] Ava Chen: that's interesting — could map to SLO burn rates. okay. any other items?

[24:15] No response.

[24:20] Ava Chen: thanks everyone, we'll re-convene after the first drafts. recording and notes will be shared.

End transcript.

source.transcript:
Meeting header: Date: 2026-09-15 | Start: 16:00 UTC | Duration: 72 minutes | Location: Zoom
Attendees: Maya, Ethan, Lina, Samir, Jordan, Priya, Noah and leadership breakout.

[00:00] Maya Patel: Alright, good afternoon everyone, thanks for hopping on. Um, we're gonna run about an hour, 15ish, try to leave time for Q&A. Quick agenda, metrics, three risks, hiring, and then open floor.

[00:22] Ethan Ross: Quick callout on infra, we've seen tail lat spikes in the EU region on long-context models. Not catastrophic but means we need to re-evaluate kernel selection for the 30k variants.

[00:45] Lina Gomez: On product velocity, Q3 launches shipped: streaming structured output, and the optimizer beta. Usage is up 24% month over month on hosted, but dedicated pipeline adoption is still slower than forecast.

[01:10] Maya Patel: Right. Noah, can you run through the top-line numbers?

[01:18] Noah Riley: Sure — revenue ARR cadence still tracking +18% QoQ, but gross margin pressure from spot GPU costs and preemption events; we're seeing a 1.6% higher cost per token than our plan. Cash runway is fine but if dedicated sales don't accelerate we need to tighten discretionary spend.

[01:50] Samir Kapoor: On the engineering side, we've got three workstreams: EU latency mitigation, batching improvements for small-seq inference, and private deploy onboarding automation. The latter is behind by about two sprints because of the VPC edge cases.

[02:15] Jordan Blake: From CS, the top two enterprise customers want explicit audit logs and SSO assurances before renewing, also asking for dedicated capacity as a path to contract expansion. We're coordinating with Product on an audit-log parity doc.

[02:40] Priya Nair: Hiring update: we closed 6 IC roles but we're still short on infra SREs and a senior infra engineer. Onboarding lead time is ~6 weeks which means hires need to be in by mid-October to impact Q4.

[03:05] Maya Patel: Okay, three risk items we're flagging: 1) EU latency tail and capacity constraints, 2) slower Dedicated conversions hitting revenue plan, 3) a potential audit backlog for customers in heavily regulated verticals. For each, we need owners and mitigation plans.

[03:30] Ethan Ross: For risk 1, engineering will do a knob-turn: kernel pinning + rollout of a region-specific fallback model. Can ship a patch-week plan, target 2 weeks.

[03:50] Samir Kapoor: I'll own the patch + runbooks and we need a canary deployment across low-volume customers first. Ops involvement required.

[04:10] Maya Patel: Risk 2 — Lina and Jordan, product+cs to define incentives for pilot customers to switch to Dedicated, include a pricing credit and an SLA trial. Can we scope a two-week pilot template?

[04:28] Lina Gomez: Yes, we have a template but need pricing confirmation from Noah. We'll iterate.

[04:40] Noah Riley: I'll model the pilot economics by end of next week.

[04:55] Jordan Blake: Risk 3 — security/compliance. We've got a queue for SOC2 evidence for three large accounts. We should run a tabletop to ensure readiness for audit asks.

[05:10] Priya Nair: Also note potential people risk — if hiring stalls we won't hit support SLAs. There's a ripple to CS and Ops.

[05:28] Maya Patel: Good. Now the OKRs refresh: product OKR 2.1 — reduce median latency 15% and 95th percentile by 25% in EU for long context models. Engineering to submit a remediation plan by 2026-09-29.

[05:48] Samir Kapoor: We'll include KV cache changes and batching window optimizations. Also, cost forecasting: we can trade a little latency for lower GPU utilization, but we need an explicit guardrail for user-facing SLAs.

[06:12] Lina Gomez: On product roadmap, we're delaying the deep quantization rollout by one sprint to align with private deployment tests. This reduces immediate risk of regressions for high-sensitivity customers.

[06:35] Maya Patel: Hiring — Priya, give us a plan to accelerate the SRE search.

[06:44] Priya Nair: We'll ramp recruiter outreach, prioritize the outbound list, and expedite interview loops. Target: 4 hires by 2026-10-15. If we can't, we move to contractor hires for the short term.

[07:05] Noah Riley: Finance asks for a reforecast: if Dedicated conversion lags by 2 quarters, we need a contingency budget to cut nonessential infra experiments. I'll present scenario plans.

[07:24] Maya Patel: Communication: leadership should be briefed with an exec one-pager on these three risks. I want it by Friday EOD.

[07:36] Ethan Ross: On infrastructure action, note that a vendor kernel update this week may improve tail latencies, but the patch has a compatibility caveat for some quantized kernels — we'll validate in staging.

[07:54] Jordan Blake: Customer notes — two reference accounts are willing to trial Dedicated if we can guarantee a migration runbook and a comms playbook. That's big for pipeline.

[08:12] Lina Gomez: We'll build the runbook and pre-populate a comms template with expected downtime and rollback steps.

[08:35] Audience Q&A (mix of manager voices):
[08:37] Manager 1: Any changes to comp or variable comp given we might miss quota?

[08:42] Maya Patel: Not at this moment; we'll wait until reforecast. We prefer non-disruptive measures first.

[08:55] Manager 2: For the EU latency patch, do we need customer opt-in?

[09:02] Samir Kapoor: No, it's server-side. We'll notify customers and provide an opt-out only if needed.

[09:20] Manager 3: Is there an updated timeline for the SOC2 materials?

[09:27] Jordan Blake: We're compiling evidence now; target to have the primary pack in two weeks.

[09:40] Maya Patel: Alright final recap — owners and high-level due dates: engineering patch for EU latency (Samir) — two weeks; pilot Dedicated template + pricing (Lina & Noah) — one week for pricing, two for pilot template; SOC2 tabletop (Jordan & Security) — schedule next week; hiring acceleration (Priya) — hiring targets by 2026-10-15; finance reforecast (Noah) — scenario plans by next Tuesday.

[10:15] Samir Kapoor: I'll schedule the canary and post the runbook in #infra-ops.

[10:28] Maya Patel: Thank you all. We'll run a follow-up next Thursday to check status. Meeting adjourned.

[10:40] End of recording.

Notes: Transcript contains minor recognition errors; names may be transcribed slightly off in some sections.

source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-07-02
Start: 16:00 PT
Duration: ~72 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen, Diego Alvarez, Marcus Lee, Nora Patel, Samir Kapoor, Priya Rao, Lian Wu + company-wide

Auto-summary (auto-gen, may be noisy):
- Quick incident recap, some numbers, apologies and thanks to on-call.
- Roadmap tweaks to prioritize reliability and cost efficiency.
- Hack week delivered two low-level kernel optim and a new batching prototype.
- Hiring steady but careful; freeze on non-critical reqs for two weeks.

[00:00:05] Ava Chen: All right, good afternoon, everyone. thanks for joining, I know it's a full calendar week. Quick note, we'll try to keep this tight, 60 a 70 minutes, we'll cover incidents, then Diego and Samir will go through ops stuff, Nora on product priorities and then Q&A.

[00:00:28] Diego Alvarez: Hey folks, quick housekeeping — apologies for the heavy slide deck, we'll post the link. Um, first off big thanks to the on-call teams, last Friday's latency spikes, uh, were messy but we got through it.

[00:00:44] Samir Kapoor: yeah, it was a thundery Friday, um, root cause was a cascade between autoscaler thrashing and a rare scheduler path — and kv cache, the kv cash warming was slower than we'd predicted. that slowed down cold starts.

[00:01:10] Marcus Lee: to add, the continuous batching queue also hit a pathological sequence that made a bunch of requests coalesce in a way that created latency microbursts, so two orthogonal issues.

[00:01:32] Nora Patel: And to be clear, customer-visible impact was about 8 minutes of elevated 95p latency in us-west-2, and SLO heatmap shows SLA blips in multi-tenant pools.

[00:01:50] Audience: (murmurs, ack)

[00:02:05] Ava Chen: Thank you. Samir, what's the tl;dr for mitigation and the short-term change?

[00:02:12] Samir Kapoor: We deployed three patches: one to stabilize the autoscaler cooldowns, one to force eager kv cache warming for pinned endpoints, and a kernel-level guard that retries lightweight scheduling paths. short term: fewer thrash windows. mid term: we have a sim run to catch these patterns.

[00:02:35] Lian Wu: The sim's already flagged two additional sequences that we hadn't seen in prod but could; we'll incorporate those into the synthetic tests.

[00:02:48] Diego Alvarez: the important callout is we need better observability into the scheduler internals; current tracing is noisy and expensive, so we'll add sparse debug traces for the next two weeks.

[00:03:05] Audience Question (text): will Dedicated customers be affected?

[00:03:10] Marcus Lee: Most Dedicated pools were fine, because of isolation, but teams that had mixed workloads on dedicated hardware with bursty multi-tenant requests saw some ripple. overall, Dedicated still gives stronger isolation, but not magic.

[00:03:35] Ava Chen: That is a nice segue to the Dedicated vs Private discussion — Nora?

[00:03:41] Nora Patel: With Dedicated we can guarantee capacity and tail latency better, but cost goes up; Private gives you network isolation and KMS control but adds ops burden. During hack week we tried a hybrid proof: pin model variants to Dedicated while routing lower-priority traffic to Hosted with aggressive batching — the metrics look promising for tail latency reduction at 12% higher unit cost but 25% lower overall p99. weird math but it's real.

[00:04:18] Samir Kapoor: small note, when I say KV cash warming earlier I meant KV cache, the transcriber is doing its thing—

[00:04:25] (laughter)

[00:04:40] Diego Alvarez: Hack Week highlights — two wins. One: patch in the runtime that reduces attention kernel memory churn, lower peak memory by around 10–12% on the 70B family. Two: a batching scheduler prototype that reduces per-token overhead by doing more aggressive pooling for short sequences. both are experimental.

[00:05:10] Audience: applause (virtual)

[00:05:25] Marcus Lee: The kernel patch requires careful vetting, we can't ship it to all fleets without more perf testing. it's sensitive because it's touching memory allocator paths. we'll run it in canary region next.

[00:05:40] Nora Patel: Also product implications: If kernel patch lands, we can lower the default reservation for some Dedicated SKUs, opening a new price tier. That's a direct revenue motion if latency stays within SLOs.

[00:06:05] Ava Chen: Hiring and budget — short summary: hiring continues for critical infra, reliability, and SRE hires. We're pausing non-critical requisitions for two weeks to re-assess headcount plan and runway. we want to be careful but continue to invest in ops.

[00:06:30] Emily Nguyen (People) [Speaker 7]: We'll send a note to managers about the temporary freeze and which roles are exempt.

[00:06:40] Audience: (quiet)

[00:06:50] Priya Rao: Security updates: no customer data exfiltration, audit logs look clean. We are accelerating KMS/HSM roadmap for Private customers — that includes integration tests with AWS KMS and a modular HSM shimming layer. we'll need product to prioritize the KMS API proposal.

[00:07:10] Nora Patel: we'll slot that into next sprint planning, Priya.

[00:07:20] Ava Chen: OK next, a little bit of transparency on metrics — Marcus?

[00:07:24] Marcus Lee: Quick numbers: overall token volume up 23% q/q, p95 latency up 6% y/y but p99 has been more volatile — the incident pushed it up. Unit cost per token is down slightly on average but variance has grown. We'll post a deeper metrics dashboard.

[00:07:50] Audience: (questions start popping in chat)

[00:08:05] Audience Question (chat): Is there a plan to change the SLA for hosted customers?

[00:08:10] Ava Chen: Short answer — not changing SLA yet. We're focused on operational fixes. If we find recurring patterns we'll revisit SLAs but we'll communicate first.

[00:08:28] Diego Alvarez: Quick demo of the new observability page — (screen share stumbles) — ok, so here's a glimpse: per-request trace sampling, scheduler state timeline, and a new 'rollback on bloom' button — it's early.

[00:09:00] Samir Kapoor: We also added a runbook template for incidents: page explains triage flow, who paged whom, escalation path, and quick rollback steps. This should shorten MTTR.

[00:09:22] Lian Wu: We're also going to run monthly chaos windows in staging to surface these autoscale-edge cases. please expect a calendar invite.

[00:09:35] Audience: (ack)

[00:09:50] Nora Patel: Product prioritization: three things move to top of roadmap in July: 1) KMS integration and audit logging for Private, 2) Autoscale policy engine with the new sim, 3) Dedicated pricing tier experiment if kernel patches validate.

[00:10:12] Audience Question (live): how will this affect existing customers on Dedicated?

[00:10:20] Marcus Lee: minimal disruption if we do proper canaries; we plan to keep Dedicated customers on pinned model images until they're validated. But we'll give advance notice.

[00:10:40] Ava Chen: housekeeping: we will hold an ops deep-dive for product and solutions engineers next Wednesday. Expect invites.

[00:10:50] (crowd chatter)

[00:11:02] Audience Question: any defcon-level changes to security posture?

[00:11:07] Priya Rao: no, nothing drastic. we're accelerating some control plane logging and PII tagging in telemetry, and we have an upcoming external audit the week of July 20th.

[00:11:20] Ava Chen: Great. I want to open for a couple of live questions from the floor — please be concise.

[00:11:35] Speaker: Hi, quick question about the simulator Lian mentioned — how accurate is it and what's the false positive rate?

[00:11:44] Lian Wu: good question. right now false positives are around 8% in the latest sim runs — meaning sequences the sim flags as risky but never hit prod patterns — but the false negative rate is the more important one, and that's below 3% for the classes we've modeled. not perfect but improving.

[00:12:05] Marcus Lee: We're injecting synthetic traces and replaying historical traffic to validate; the sim's configurability allows us to tune for workloads.

[00:12:20] Audience: (short clap)

[00:12:35] Nora Patel: Quick note on communication: we'll post a customer-facing status update template for any future rolling degradations. Ops will lead on content, Product will sign off.

[00:12:50] Diego Alvarez: Another technical note— when we say "kernel" I'm talking about inference runtime kernels, not OS kernels; I know the transcriber will confuse that later.

[00:13:05] (chuckle)

[00:13:15] Ava Chen: I want to be explicit — thank you to the on-call, thank you to teams who pivoted during hack week. This is the work that keeps our customers trusting us. We'll keep heads-down on ops and come back in two weeks with measurable progress.

[00:13:32] Audience Poll (chat): thumbs up if you think the incident handling was OK — mixed reactions.

[00:13:45] Q&A continues (selected):
[00:13:50] Question: when are we lifting the hiring freeze?

[00:13:56] Ava Chen: We plan a review in two weeks after we've seen the headcount reprioritization; exceptions for critical infra roles only.

[00:14:10] Question: can product give more detail on Dedicated pricing experiments?

[00:14:16] Nora Patel: We'll publish an initial proposal and model scenarios by next Tuesday — it's not final.

[00:14:30] Diego Alvarez: On the experimental batching: note it changes latency distribution for short sequences — some short requests might wait slightly longer for batching to fill. We'll expose a knob to opt out per-route.

[00:14:50] Audience: (concerned)

[00:15:05] Marcus Lee: For customers who can't tolerate changes, keep them on pinned Dedicated instances or mark routes as low-latency opt-out.

[00:15:20] Ava Chen: All right, folks, we're approaching the midway mark — lots of questions — we appreciate the engagement. Final quick lightning updates from each leader — 1 minute max. Diego?

[00:15:33] Diego Alvarez: Pushed the kernel patch to canary in us-east-1, monitoring. Also we're creating a lightweight tracing mode to be enabled on-demand, that will be in the console soon.

[00:15:50] Samir Kapoor: Incident postmortem doc will be published today with timeline, and we'll run a blameless review.

source.transcript:
Meeting header
Date: 2026-06-23
Start: 14:00 UTC
Duration: ~75m
Location: Zoom (internal)
Attendees: Priya (PM), Owen (Eng Mgr), Diego (Infra SE), Ava (EngOps), Sam (SRE), Taylor (QA), Marta (Prod Ops), Luis (Telemetry), Zoe (Release), Nikhil (Security), Maya (VP Product), Kristin (CTO), Ben (Finance), Leo (CS)

Summary: Quick tabletop-style simulation of a deploy that triggers staged degradation; align on guardrails, triggers, telemetry, and exec handoff. Focus on rollback thresholds and who owns user-visible comms.

Topics: runbook, canary sizing, staged degradation tiers, KV cache hotstart, model fallback variants, security/KMS rotations, telemetry dashboards, finance risk for reserved capacity.

[00:00] Priya: okay, uh, hi everyone thanks for joining, we've got a tight agenda — walk a short tabletop of the deploy that we've been planning, um, simulate a degradation where latency climbs and kv cache misses spike and then talk owner handoffs to execs.
[00:22] Maya (VP Product): thanks, Priya. My ask is basically can we show, in one slide, what happens to latency and cost if we fall back from a larger model to the quantized variant and then to the smaller family. Execs wanna know visible impact.
[00:36] Owen: yup, we'll show two curves — token latency and p95 — and a triage path. Quick note, our fallback path has three tiers: same-arch quantized, smaller-arch v2, and last resort hosted-safe mode with lower throughput.
[00:51] Diego: and just to flag, the KV cache warm-up will be the noisy bit. If the prefix cache cold-starts, you'll see a token cost bump and p95 spike, that's our likely first signal.
[01:12] Ava: what are we calling the signal? Is it cache miss ratio or user-facing 95th? We need something with low false positives.
[01:25] Sam: i'd prefer a combined signal: p95 > 500ms for three minutes AND cache miss ratio > 20% over same window. We can tune thresholds later but that's a start.
[01:40] Taylor: QA will need to run degraded-mode tests that target those thresholds; we'll generate synthetic traffic that simulates cold prefixes.
[01:52] Priya: good. ok simulate step one — a spike at t+10m post-deploy where latency rises, flow?
[02:03] Zoe: Release note — during canary we'll only route 5% of traffic for 30m, so we expect to catch issues early if thresholds fire. But the canary config will be on the same infra pool as dedicated workloads, so we need clear isolation rules.
[02:21] Kristin (CTO): isolation is essential, we can't risk noisy neighbor cross-talk. Diego, what's the plan for hard isolation?
[02:31] Diego: for this canary we put a quota of GPU slices and set run-to-completion queues with eviction. If it overruns we'll move canary traffic to a separate pool and fallback to the hosted-safe path. The eviction is aggressive but necessary.
[02:51] Marta: who owns the decision to move canary traffic? Is that automated or manual? Execs want a brief on that.
[03:02] Owen: automation with human guardrails — we'll auto-degrade to fallback variant but only auto-roll back after human acknowledgements for production. For canary we can automate rollback.
[03:15] Ben (Finance): quick ask — what's the cost delta if we auto-degrade vs rollback? Finance needs a sense of committed spend risk.
[03:27] Luis: i'll publish a dashboard showing token cost per model family and delta per minute. Short story: auto-degrade increases unit cost for smaller model? actually reduces cost per token but might increase latency — the cost impact depends on routing.
[03:44] Priya: let's step through the simulated timeline. T0 deploy, t+5m canary sees first anomalies, t+10m threshold triggers, t+15m staged fallback kicks in, t+20m exec notif.
[03:58] [04:00] Priya: we should roleplay the comms — who calls out to clients? Leo?
[04:06] Leo (CS): we'll draft a customer-facing note but for internal exec notif, Marta will send the one-pager; CS will prepare an operational blurb.
[04:18] Marta: confirmed. We'll use status.redwood.internal and tag impacted customers if their SLOs are affected.
[04:29] Nikhil (Security): KMS note — if we switch to hosted-safe we also need to ensure any customer-managed keys remain unavailable; double-check with legal for exceptions.
[04:40] Sam: noted. Also, the KV cache uses ephemeral encryption but the telemetry bucket is encrypted with the HSM. We're okay but rotation windows might cause small blips — avoid rotation during canary.
[04:55] Priya: action: Nikhil — confirm rotation blackout window for canary.
[04:59] Nikhil: will do, target by next Monday.
[05:05] [05:30] Taylor: outline of QA degraded-mode tests — we'll run three scenarios: cold-prefix heavy, high-concurrency short-bursts, and model-fallback correctness. The correctness pass must assert no hallucination regressions beyond the agreed threshold.
[05:50] Owen: define hallucination threshold again? We used delta-LM perplexity last time but execs ask for a simpler quality metric.
[06:03] Priya: pragmatic metric — response divergence score against golden prompts; QA to provide percent of prompts that exceed tolerance. We'll accept up to 5% degradation for fallback variants during canary for non-critical flows.
[06:18] Taylor: noted. We'll include that in test plan.
[06:25] [06:45] Diego: technical caveat — when we switch model families there's a KV cache mismatch. We either purge prefix-specific cache or map keys, both have tradeoffs. Purging causes immediate cold starts but mapping is brittle.
[07:01] Ava: is there a hybrid? Maybe warm a small sample of prefixes into the new cache after we route traffic but before full switch.
[07:11] Diego: yeah, a warm-up window with simulated requests could reduce p95 spike. We'll script a prefix mellowing job.
[07:22] Owen: that means release team needs to kick-off a warm-up job as part of the canary pipeline. Zoe you'll own that step in the runbook.
[07:33] Zoe: got it — I will add a pre-switch warm-up step.
[07:38] [07:50] Priya: what about observability? Luis, do we have a dashboard that shows the three-tier fallback and direct users to rollback signal?
[07:59] Luis: i'm putting together a single-page dashboard: p95, kv-miss, cost/min, model-version mix, and an 'alert severity' field. I can add an explicit 'rollback suggested' boolean based on threshold logic.
[08:13] Priya: perfect. We'll use that as the canonical source for execs.
[08:18] [08:30] Ben: will reserved capacity exposure be material if canary fails and we need to burst? Finance wants to know if we'll exceed our reserved GPU commit.
[08:38] Owen: we have burst pools but they cost more; we'll auto-switch to hosted-safe only if reserve is low — that's a routing policy. Priya, we'll include policies in the slide.
[08:50] [09:00] Priya: ok i'd like to simulate the failure path now — assume the warm-up didn't reduce miss ratio and p95 stayed above 650ms for 10 minutes. What do we do?
[09:12] Sam: at that point we escalate to Stage 2: fallback to quantized variant across canary and pause further rollout. We notify execs and open incident channel. If metrics don't recover in 15m, rollback.
[09:26] Taylor: QA needs to capture the state pre-fallback so we can triage whether regression is model-quality or infra. We'll snapshot logs and the last 100 requests.
[09:38] [09:55] Zoe: rollback mechanics — we have two rollback types: config rollback (traffic routing back to old model) and infra rollback (re-provision previous pool). We'll try config rollback first since it's fastest.
[10:09] Owen: yes, config rollback should be under 3 minutes if the control plane is healthy; if control plane degraded we may need manual infra steps.
[10:19] Nikhil: security note — ensure any rollback doesn't leak private keys; double check that old and new variants adhere to the same key access pattern.
[10:30] [10:45] Priya: good. Now stakeholder handoff: what does execs want in the post-mortem if we do rollback? Maya?
[10:52] Maya: short bullet list — cause, impact, customer count, duration, and next mitigation steps. Keep it under one page. Also, highlight mitigation budget impacts if we had to burst.
[11:05] Ben: finance will need cost impact in dollars for the incident window and forecast change. Luis, coordinate with me on the data feed.
[11:14] Luis: will do. I'll add a 'cost-per-minute' timeseries for the incident window.
[11:21] [11:35] Marta: who publishes the post-mortem? Is it Product or Incident Lead?
[11:41] Owen: incident lead (Sam) should own the post-mortem writeup with PM and Prod Ops sign-off.
[11:48] Sam: agree, I'll set the template and Priya will sign off.
[11:54] [12:05] Priya: okay let's run a very short roleplay: Sam you'll open the channel after the stage-1 trigger; Marta you'll prepare the one-pager, Zoe you run the warm-up pre-switch and release steps, Nikhil confirm KMS blackout. Taylor run the degraded tests, Luis prep dashboard.
[12:18] Sam: opening incident channel... (simulated) ok done.
[12:23] Marta: drafting the one-pager now (simulated).
[12:28] Taylor: starting degraded mode tests (simulated).
[12:34] [12:45] Diego: quick note, we should add a grace metric for longer-running requests — some customer flows are batch-ish and spike p95 legitimately. We need to annotate those routes so they don't trigger false positives.
[12:58] Priya: good point. Action for Zoe + Diego to annotate critical routes and ensure threshold exceptions.
[13:06] Zoe: will add route annotations to the canary config.
[13:10] [13:20] Owen: one more infrastructure caveat — the quantized variant needs different kernel selection at deploy-time; if we don't pre-warm kernels we'll hit slow first calls. Diego, can you ensure kernel boot is part of warm-up?
[13:33] Diego: yes we'll include a kernel handshake step.
[13:37] [13:50] Priya: ok nearing end — recap of owners and deliverables, and the slide for execs. Priya: i'll draft the slide; Owen and Luis will add data slices; Marta will schedule handoff meeting with execs after we validate dashboard.
[14:05] Maya: agree, prefer the handoff to happen only after telemetry is live.
[14:12] Ben: finance sitting on the sideline until we see the reserve exposure numbers.
[14:19] Priya: final wrap, any last comments?
[14:24] Nikhil: just a thumbs up, i'll confirm KMS window by Monday.
[14:29] Leo: customer comms template will be ready once we have the one-pager.
[14:33] [14:40] Priya: thanks everyone, i'll follow up with notes and ping on backlog. Meeting adjourned.

Post-meeting notes:
- Decision model: three-stage fallback, auto-degrade -> canary rollback -> manual production rollback.
- Primary signals: p95, kv-cache-miss%, model mix shift, cost/min.
- Exec one-pager and single-page dashboard agreed as canonical artifacts.
- QA will run degraded scenarios and provide a quality delta metric vs golden prompts.

References/links (to be shared):
- Runbook draft (link placeholder): https://internal.redwood/runbooks/deploy-guardrail-draft
- Telemetry dashboard (placeholder): https://internal.redwood/dashboards/deploy-guardrail
- QA degraded test plan (placeholder): https://internal.redwood/qa/degraded-tests
- Postmortem template: https://internal.redwood/incident/template

Transcript notes: transcript auto-generated; contains occasional mislabels and speech artifacts typical for internal call recordings.

[03:44] Priya: let's step through the simulated timeline. T0 deploy, t+5m canary sees first anomalies, t+10m threshold triggers, t+15m staged fallback kicks in, t+20m exec notif.
[03:58] [04:00] Priya: we should roleplay the comms — who calls out to clients? Leo?
[04:06] Leo (CS): we'll draft a customer-facing note but for internal exec notif, Marta will send the one-pager; CS will prepare an operational blurb.
[04:18] Marta: confirmed. We'll use status.redwood.internal and tag impacted customers if their SLOs are affected.
[04:29] Nikhil (Security): KMS note — if we switch to hosted-safe we also need to ensure any customer-managed keys remain unavailable; double-check with legal for exceptions.
[04:40] Sam: noted. Also, the KV cache uses ephemeral encryption but the telemetry bucket is encrypted with the HSM. We're okay but rotation windows might cause small blips — avoid rotation during canary.
[04:55] Priya: action: Nikhil — confirm rotation blackout window for canary.
[04:59] Nikhil: will do, target by next Monday.
[05:05] [05:30] Taylor: outline of QA degraded-mode tests — we'll run three scenarios: cold-prefix heavy, high-concurrency short-bursts, and model-fallback correctness. The correctness pass must assert no hallucination regressions beyond the agreed threshold.
[05:50] Owen: define hallucination threshold again? We used delta-LM perplexity last time but execs ask for a simpler quality metric.
[06:03] Priya: pragmatic metric — response divergence score against golden prompts; QA to provide percent of prompts that exceed tolerance. We'll accept up to 5% degradation for fallback variants during canary for non-critical flows.
[06:18] Taylor: noted. We'll include that in test plan.
[06:25] [06:45] Diego: technical caveat — when we switch model families there's a KV cache mismatch. We either purge prefix-specific cache or map keys, both have tradeoffs. Purging causes immediate cold starts but mapping is brittle.
[07:01] Ava: is there a hybrid? Maybe warm a small sample of prefixes into the new cache after we route traffic but before full switch.
[07:11] Diego: yeah, a warm-up window with simulated requests could reduce p95 spike. We'll script a prefix mellowing job.
[07:22] Owen: that means release team needs to kick-off a warm-up job as part of the canary pipeline. Zoe you'll own that step in the runbook.
[07:33] Zoe: got it — I will add a pre-switch warm-up step.
[07:38] [07:50] Priya: what about observability? Luis, do we have a dashboard that shows the three-tier fallback and direct users to rollback signal?
[07:59] Luis: i'm putting together a single-page dashboard: p95, kv-miss, cost/min, model-version mix, and an 'alert severity' field. I can add an explicit 'rollback suggested' boolean based on threshold logic.
[08:13] Priya: perfect. We'll use that as the canonical source for execs.
[08:18] [08:30] Ben: will reserved capacity exposure be material if canary fails and we need to burst? Finance wants to know if we'll exceed our reserved GPU commit.
[08:38] Owen: we have burst pools but they cost more; we'll auto-switch to hosted-safe only if reserve is low — that's a routing policy. Priya, we'll include policies in the slide.
[08:50] [09:00] Priya: ok i'd like to simulate the failure path now — assume the warm-up didn't reduce miss ratio and p95 stayed above 650ms for 10 minutes. What do we do?
[09:12] Sam: at that point we escalate to Stage 2: fallback to quantized variant across canary and pause further rollout. We notify execs and open incident channel. If metrics don't recover in 15m, rollback.
[09:26] Taylor: QA needs to capture the state pre-fallback so we can triage whether regression is model-quality or infra. We'll snapshot logs and the last 100 requests.
[09:38] [09:55] Zoe: rollback mechanics — we have two rollback types: config rollback (traffic routing back to old model) and infra rollback (re-provision previous pool). We'll try config rollback first since it's fastest.
[10:09] Owen: yes, config rollback should be under 3 minutes if the control plane is healthy; if control plane degraded we may need manual infra steps.
[10:19] Nikhil: security note — ensure any rollback doesn't leak private keys; double check that old and new variants adhere to the same key access pattern.
[10:30] [10:45] Priya: good. Now stakeholder handoff: what does execs want in the post-mortem if we do rollback? Maya?
[10:52] Maya: short bullet list — cause, impact, customer count, duration, and next mitigation steps. Keep it under one page. Also, highlight mitigation budget impacts if we had to burst.
[11:05] Ben: finance will need cost impact in dollars for the incident window and forecast change. Luis, coordinate with me on the data feed.
[11:14] Luis: will do. I'll add a 'cost-per-minute' timeseries for the incident window.
[11:21] [11:35] Marta: who publishes the post-mortem? Is it Product or Incident Lead?
[11:41] Owen: incident lead (Sam) should own the post-mortem writeup with PM and Prod Ops sign-off.
[11:48] Sam: agree, I'll set the template and Priya will sign off.
[11:54] [12:05] Priya: okay let's run a very short roleplay: Sam you'll open the channel after the stage-1 trigger; Marta you'll prepare the one-pager, Zoe you run the warm-up pre-switch and release steps, Nikhil confirm KMS blackout. Taylor run the degraded tests, Luis prep dashboard.
[12:18] Sam: opening incident channel... (simulated) ok done.
[12:23] Marta: drafting the one-pager now (simulated).
[12:28] Taylor: starting degraded mode tests (simulated).
[12:34] [12:45] Diego: quick note, we should add a grace metric for longer-running requests — some customer flows are batch-ish and spike p95 legitimately. We need to annotate those routes so they don't trigger false positives.
[12:58] Priya: good point. Action for Zoe + Diego to annotate critical routes and ensure threshold exceptions.
[13:06] Zoe: will add route annotations to the canary config.
[13:10] [13:20] Owen: one more infrastructure caveat — the quantized variant needs different kernel selection at deploy-time; if we don't pre-warm kernels we'll hit slow first calls. Diego, can you ensure kernel boot is part of warm-up?
[13:33] Diego: yes we'll include a kernel handshake step.
[13:37] [13:50] Priya: ok nearing end — recap of owners and deliverables, and the slide for execs. Priya: i'll draft the slide; Owen and Luis will add data slices; Marta will schedule handoff meeting with execs after we validate dashboard.
[14:05] Maya: agree, prefer the handoff to happen only after telemetry is live.
[14:12] Ben: finance sitting on the sideline until we see the reserve exposure numbers.
[14:19] Priya: final wrap, any last comments?
[14:24] Nikhil: just a thumbs up, i'll confirm KMS window by Monday.
[14:29] Leo: customer comms template will be ready once we have the one-pager.
[14:33] [14:40] Priya: thanks everyone, i'll follow up with notes and ping on backlog. Meeting adjourned.

Post-meeting notes:
- Decision model: three-stage fallback, auto-degrade -> canary rollback -> manual production rollback.
- Primary signals: p95, kv-cache-miss%, model mix shift, cost/min.
- Exec one-pager and single-page dashboard agreed as canonical artifacts.
- QA will run degraded scenarios and provide a quality delta metric vs golden prompts.

References/links (to be shared):
- Runbook draft (link placeholder): https://internal.redwood/runbooks/deploy-guardrail-draft
- Telemetry dashboard (placeholder): https://internal.redwood/dashboards/deploy-guardrail
- QA degraded test plan (placeholder): https://internal.redwood/qa/degraded-tests
- Postmortem template: https://internal.redwood/incident/template
