customer_attendees: Product, Eng, Sales reps tuned in
topics: SLA and security posture progress (SOC 2, KMS)
Cost/latency targets and Optimize feature rollout
Operational readiness for EU region launch
source.summary: Leadership huddle covering hiring cadence, upcoming Dedicated capacity milestones, private deployment onboarding, and an ops-first checklist for the next two sprints. CEO opens with company context and growth metrics. CTO and VP Eng sketch performance wins and remaining risks (tail latency, batching), Product describes Redwood Optimize beta rollout. People/Revenue update on hiring and customer health. Q&A focuses on EU region readiness, SOC 2 timelines, and what counts as success for the trial customers.
source.transcript:
[00:00] Ava Chen: Good morning everyone, thanks for joining — a short leadership huddle, we want to cover hires, product milestones, and a quick ops checklist. Uh, Marcus if you want to start with the infra notes?
[00:20] Marcus Rivera: Yeah thanks Ava. Quick snapshot — latencies are down 12% p90 across hosted since the kernel switch last month, so big kudos to infra. We still have tail spikes — intermittent — when we see large seq lengths plus bursty small requests. Looks like batching didn't catch a few of the short tail flows, so we're tuning the continuous batching window.
[00:45] Priya Nair: When you say kernel switch, is that the new quantized path or the cu attent thing? I saw notes in the console about "quantized runtime" but the ticket used a shorthand that got me confused.
[00:55] Marcus Rivera: Right, so two items — the quantized execution path and a patch for attention kernel selection. The console label was "Qx-kern" which is why it looked weird in the changelog. Sorry about the naming.
[01:10] Ethan Brooks: On the ops side we're seeing good throughput on Dedicated in the staging clusters — 1.8x better for the 8k seq models after the batching tune. However, for Private (VPC/on-prem) installs, the onboarding script failed on one of the customer's firewalls because our installer assumes outbound metadata calls. We're adding an HTTP-less bootstrap mode.
[01:35] Ava Chen: That's important for regulated customers. Sofia how are hiring numbers looking against the plan?
[01:40] Sofia Alvarez: We're slightly behind on mid-senior infra hires — 4 offers out, 2 accepted, 2 still in loop. We also have high withdraw risk in one candidate because of counteroffers. People is recommending more compensation flexibility for a hiring window. Also planning manager workshops next month to help scale IC->EM transitions.
[02:05] Janelle Park: From GTM, NRR for the quarter is holding at 102% but the top-of-funnel conversion dipped on the self-serve trial for Dedicated because some customers can't test reserved capacity on small accounts. Sales suggests a lightweight "demo capacity" credit to surface performance wins.
[02:30] Priya Nair: Product update — Redwood Optimize beta is live for 10 customers. Initial feedback: caching suggestions are good, but the UI needs clearer messaging around safety tradeoffs when we propose quantization profiles. We should add a "regressions?" quick link to tie evals to the suggested config.
[02:55] Marcus Rivera: Agreed; also we need a playbook: if Optimize suggests a 4-bit quantization, the SE must run a perf-quality regression with the customer's critical prompt set. We can automate the smoke evals but SE oversight is needed.
[03:15] Ava Chen: Let's be explicit with the field — lead SEs should get a 48 hour window to validate before applying any aggressive profile to production. Add that to the rollout steps.
[03:30] (crosstalk)
[03:35] Unknown Speaker: Yeah, and the logs — we need a token-level cost breakdown tied to a trace ID; ops asked for this in the console backlog.
[03:50] Ethan Brooks: That's on the backlog, we have a PR for token-level traces, scheduled for the 2-week sprint.
[04:05] Priya Nair: EU region launch: engineering says infra is green, but compliance wants a hard SOC 2 date. Where are we?
[04:15] Marcus Rivera: SOC 2 readiness is in progress—control mapping is done, we have gaps in incident response drill and some vendor risk assessment. Realistically, formal attestation target is end of Q3. We can provide interim artifacts for sales.
[04:35] Janelle Park: Sales needs interim security collateral — a "what-we-have-now" packet, plus an "attestation timeline" we can show to procurement.
[04:45] Sofia Alvarez: People note — when SOC 2 is announced make sure we coordinate the comms with legal and security to avoid premature claims.
[05:00] Ava Chen: Good point. Action: legal to draft the interim security pack by May 19th. Marcus, can you commit?
[05:07] Marcus Rivera: Yes, I'll put something together with Security — ETA May 18.
[05:12] (pause / shuffling papers)
[05:25] Ava Chen: On revenue and pricing — we had a pricing tilt for Dedicated last month, early signals show churn risk for small customers who were using Dedicated to avoid on-demand costs. Janelle, what's the plan?
[05:40] Janelle Park: We're testing a tiered reserved offering with a small "starter" commitment and a clear upgrade path. Also exploring per-route throttles and quotas to prevent noisy tenants from spiking cost.
[05:55] Priya Nair: Related product ask — we should make the model routing policies first-class in the console so customers can set latency-first vs cost-first on a per-route basis. It will reduce churn if customers can guarantee SLAs for certain endpoints.
[06:15] Ethan Brooks: Engineering is on board but that's a medium-term change — needs RBAC and paramized routing flags. Could be 8-10 weeks.
[06:25] Ava Chen: Okay call that Q3 initiative unless we find a lighter-weight toggle.
[06:35] (quick mic static)
[06:40] Audience question (Slack raised by an IC): What are the top two things leadership needs from ICs this quarter?
[06:48] Ava Chen: One — focus on shipping the EU readiness items (region infra, telemetry, and interim security docs). Two — improve alerting fidelity so SREs can find real issues not noise.
[07:05] Priya Nair: Also, please keep eval testbeds updated for Optimize — stale datasets are causing bad recommendations.
[07:18] (murmur)
[07:25] Ethan Brooks: Ops note: onboarding script for Private will gain a new flag --bootstrap-local to avoid external metadata calls; we think it will unblock two ongoing POCs.
[07:40] Marcus Rivera: On the Dedicated SLAs — we need a formal measurement plan for latency SLOs that includes client-side metrics to catch tail behavior. PMs will collaborate with SE to build the measurement harness.
[07:55] Sofia Alvarez: Hiring ask again — we have an intro-to-management curriculum draft; I want leadership feedback by May 20.
[08:05] Ava Chen: Copy that.
[08:12] (brief pause)
[08:15] Janelle Park: Customer health highlight — Customer "OrchidHealth" expanded to Dedicated after pilot, citing "predictable latency and cost"; their CSM says the rollout was smoother than expected. Good story for sales.
[08:30] Priya Nair: Quick product note — we'll freeze Optimize's public UI for the next fortnight while we instrument the safety/regression hooks. Internal beta continues.
[08:45] Ava Chen: Ok we'll take questions.
[08:50] Audience Q: Is private on-prem support going to require a different support SKU?
[08:55] Janelle Park: Yes likely a premium support SKU with on-call rotation for infra-level incidents. We'll draft options by next week.
[09:05] Audience Q: EU data residency — do we have KMS in-region?
[09:10] Marcus Rivera: Not yet — KMS regionalization is scheduled as part of the EU launch workstream, vendor procurement is next step.
[09:20] Ava Chen: Final quick recap: legal to produce interim security packet (Marcus + Legal) due May 18–19; SEs to validate Optimize quant profiles for active pilots within 48 hours of suggestion; People to share management curriculum draft by May 20; Engineering to ship Private bootstrap flag in next sprint; Sales to prepare small reserved starter credits experiment and collateral.
[09:55] (voices confirming)
[10:00] Ava Chen: Alright, close out with gratitude — big quarter ahead. We'll follow up with the detailed notes and assignment sheet. Thanks all.
[10:10] (meeting ends)

source.title: Internal: Metering sanity checks kickoff (Finance + Eng + Product)
source.summary: Kickoff meeting to align on the "meter sanity" initiative: define what we will monitor (freshness, drift vs ledger, duplicates/idempotency, null dims like region/model, cache-hit attribution), agree on owners and escalation, and set a 6–8 week timeline with a shadow-billing compare window. Finance called out month-end close constraints (usage freeze window, no retro invoice changes without sign-off). Engineering outlined current failure modes (double-counting on retries/fallback, embeddings batching accounting, late-arriving events) and proposed automated checks + dashboards with paging thresholds.
topics: Why meter sanity checks now (invoice disputes + revrec risk + experiment validity)
Data quality KPIs: drift, freshness, duplicates, null rates
Shadow billing compare period and change control
Month-end close usage freeze window + backfill policy
questions: What drift threshold should page vs only alert (daily and weekly)?
How do we segment experiment readouts pre/post meter fixes so conclusions stay valid?
How long do we keep a dedupe window for usage events, and what is the idempotency key source?
Do we bill cached tokens as 0, discounted, or "billed_tokens" separate from raw? (need consistent definition across endpoints)
Who signs off when a backfill changes billed totals for a closed month?
action_items: Nadia Rahman - Draft initial meter sanity KPI definitions (drift %, freshness SLA, null/dup rates) and circulate for approval - due 2026-02-25
Mei Lin - Confirm "source of truth" tables for RevRec and the exact join keys for ledger vs usage events vs customer exports - due 2026-02-26
Aisha Bello - Propose alert routing/paging thresholds (what pages SRE vs what goes to #finance-billing) and annotation format linking to runbooks - due 2026-02-27
Logan Wright - Identify top 3 known failure modes to build into canary/integration tests (streaming disconnects, retry/fallback, embeddings batch) - due 2026-02-25
Leila Farouk - Add metering regression scenarios to perf-canary and define pass/fail invariants (no double count, stable token attribution) - due 2026-03-01
Emily Stone - Document month-end close constraints: usage freeze window, late events policy, and required Finance sign-offs - due 2026-02-28
Chris Osei - Update experiment analysis plan to include explicit pre/post meter-fix segmentation and caveats section template - due 2026-02-27
Cole Summers - Confirm idempotency key strategy for billing hooks and dedupe window behavior during partial failures/backfills - due 2026-02-26
Connor O'Brien - Draft change control checklist for meter changes (shadow billing compare period, approvals, rollout gates) - due 2026-02-28
next_steps: Schedule 30 min follow-up to lock KPI thresholds + paging policy (end of week)
Start shadow-billing compare in staging as soon as KPI spec is agreed; run for at least 7 days before production changes affect invoices
Stand up first dashboard tiles: freshness lag, duplicate rate, null region/model rate, drift vs ledger by day/account/model/region
Create a single Confluence hub page linking: meter spec, reconciliation playbook, dashboard, and change control process
source.transcript:
Meeting Header
Date: 2026-02-21
Time: 10:00 AM PST
Duration: 58 min
Title: Internal: Metering sanity checks kickoff (Finance + Eng + Product)
Attendees: Talia Benson (PM), Logan Wright (Eng - Telemetry), Nadia Rahman (Staff - Metering), Mei Lin (Data/Recon), Aisha Bello (SLO/Dashboards), Emily Stone (RevRec/Accounting), Rishi Malhotra (FP&A), Chris Osei (Product Ops), Nikhil Sharma (PM Hosted API), Cole Summers (Billing Hooks), Caleb Johnson (Pipelines), Leila Farouk (Test Automation), Connor O'Brien (Release)
Notes: Fireflies transcription; may contain errors.

[00:00] Talia Benson: Alright, we’re at time. Thanks everyone. This is the kickoff for metering sanity checks. The goal today is pretty tactical: what are we checking, who owns which pieces, and what timeline we’re committing to so Finance can sleep at night and Product can trust the experiments.

[00:22] Talia Benson: Also, reminder this is internal. We’ll keep customer names out of the doc, but the drivers are the same—invoice disputes and experiment noise.

[00:33] Logan Wright: Yep. And just to frame it, we’ve had a handful of issues that are small individually—like a retry path that double counts tokens, or missing region on events—but they compound. And then Finance sees a drift vs ledger, and Support sees “my invoice doesn’t match my export.”

[00:55] Emily Stone: Yeah. From RevRec side the biggest pain is not just the dispute, it’s auditability. If we can’t explain where the number came from and prove consistency, it’s a control problem.

[01:08] Rishi Malhotra: And for FP&A, the same. Our gross margin model assumes the meters are stable. If a meter definition changes mid-quarter and we don’t segment, the conclusions are… basically garbage.

[01:22] Talia Benson: Great. I’m going to use a lightweight agenda: (1) define “meter sanity” signals, (2) source of truth / recon flow, (3) alerting + thresholds, (4) change control, shadow billing compare, (5) owners and dates.

[01:39] Nadia Rahman: Cool. I can start with the definition set. For Hosted API, we need a consistent set of fields: raw prompt tokens, raw completion tokens, and billed tokens. Then request counts. Then a way to attribute cache hits—prefix cache or KV cache—without accidentally charging twice.

[02:04] Nikhil Sharma: Small note: customers ask about “fairness” a lot. They see raw tokens in their app logs, then billed tokens in the invoice. So we need a clear story: billed tokens reflect what we processed or served, and caching can reduce billed tokens—or at least be called out.

[02:18] Nadia Rahman: Yep. And for streaming, it gets gnarly. If the client disconnects mid-stream, we still may have generated tokens. Today we have some inconsistencies about when we finalize the usage event.

[02:32] Cole Summers: And retries. There’s server-side fallback, there’s client retries with the same idempotency key, and there’s “unknown” retries where the client didn’t send a key. Dedupe windows matter.

[02:47] Talia Benson: Let’s list the sanity checks. Aisha, can you capture?

[02:52] Aisha Bello: Yep. I’m writing.

[02:55] Logan Wright: The big buckets I’d propose:
1) Freshness: time lag between request logs → usage events → ledger/export table.
2) Drift: aggregated billed usage events vs billing ledger by day, account, model, region.
3) Duplicates: duplicate usage_event_id rate, and idempotency collisions.
4) Null dimensions: region/model/endpoint missing, cache_hit missing.
5) Outliers: sudden spikes in tokens per request, or embeddings units per batch, beyond expected percentiles.

[03:29] Mei Lin: +1. Also “late arriving events” rate. Like, what percent of events for day D land after D+1. That’s what breaks month-end close.

[03:41] Emily Stone: Yes. We need a freeze window. We can do a “soft” freeze where late events go to an adjustments bucket, but we need sign-off if it changes a closed period.

[03:52] Connor O'Brien: From release side, we can enforce shadow compare. Meaning: compute old meter and new meter in parallel for X days. If diff is above threshold, it blocks promotion.

[04:07] Talia Benson: That’s the “shadow billing compare” mode. We want that as a hard gate for any meter change.

[04:14] Caleb Johnson: Just to be explicit, we need stable tables. Right now Finance is querying a mix of raw events and derived views. We should have a documented export table, versioned schema, with access controls.

[04:30] Mei Lin: Agree. For recon we need three layers: (a) usage events (immutable-ish), (b) the aggregation that produces “billable line items,” and (c) ledger entries. If we don’t know which is source of truth for what, every investigation becomes a snowflake.

[04:48] Talia Benson: Let’s talk “source of truth.” Emily, for RevRec what do you want to treat as the system of record?

[04:55] Emily Stone: The billing ledger is the record for invoicing and revenue. But we need a reliable bridge from usage events to ledger so we can explain variances. I’m not saying usage events are authoritative for dollars—ledger is. But events must reconcile.

[05:14] Mei Lin: So in practice: ledger is truth for invoiced amount; usage export table is truth for customer-facing usage API; sanity checks ensure those match within drift thresholds.

[05:27] Rishi Malhotra: On drift threshold—what are we thinking? If it’s 0.1% we’ll page constantly. If it’s 5% that’s too late.

[05:36] Aisha Bello: We can do two thresholds: warning vs paging. Like: warn at 0.5% daily drift for top accounts, page at 1% or if absolute dollar impact > some amount.

[05:51] Logan Wright: For big accounts, absolute dollars might be more relevant. Drift of 0.7% can be huge depending on volume.

[06:01] Emily Stone: We should have a materiality threshold aligned with Finance. Also, drift by dimension: account/day/model/region. Sometimes drift is localized to one model in one region because a field went null.

[06:16] Nadia Rahman: Region going null is a known one. We had a path where OpenAI compat endpoint was not attaching region reliably. It’s basically a missing dimension problem.

[06:30] Nikhil Sharma: And it hits pricing experiments because region maps to cost and margin assumptions, so the cohort attribution gets messed up.

[06:39] Chris Osei: Yep. For experiment readouts, we need a flag in the dataset that indicates “meter version.” Then we can split analyses pre/post fix. Otherwise we’ll chase phantom lift.

[06:52] Talia Benson: I want to lock that: every readout includes a “meter change overlap” section. Chris, you own that template.

[07:01] Chris Osei: Got it.

[07:03] Talia Benson: Now, can we talk through the timeline? The project doc says 6–8 weeks. What does week 1-2 actually mean for us?

[07:13] Logan Wright: Week 1-2: audit + discrepancy sampling. Basically: pick a set of accounts and days, compare ledger vs usage events vs customer exports. Identify top failure modes.

[07:25] Mei Lin: I can drive the sampling queries, but I need agreement on which tables. There’s like three “usage_agg” views right now.

[07:34] Caleb Johnson: I’ll help. We can pick one, document it, and deprecate the others.

[07:41] Emily Stone: And we need a sign-off trail. Like, if we decide “this is the recon table for close,” it should be documented.

[07:50] Connor O'Brien: Week 2-4: implement fixes + tests + exports + dashboards. I’d like to run shadow compare in week 3 at the latest.

[08:02] Leila Farouk: For tests, I’ll need reproductions. Like, streaming disconnect scenario: if the client drops at token 200, we should bill 200 generated tokens, not 400 because of retry.

[08:17] Logan Wright: We have some repro notes. Nadia and I can provide the three main ones.

[08:24] Nadia Rahman: I’ll share the edge case list. It includes tool calls too—function calling sometimes looks like “tool tokens” and gets attributed wrong.

[08:36] Cole Summers: And embeddings batching. That’s a big one. The request can include multiple inputs, and we need to account for all of them without double counting per batch.

[08:48] Talia Benson: So, deliverables: KPI spec, recon playbook, automated drift checks, dashboard, alerts, plus shadow compare before impacting invoices.

[09:00] Aisha Bello: For dashboards, do we want it in Grafana, Console, or both?

[09:06] Logan Wright: Both eventually. Grafana for on-call and raw signals, Console for Finance/RevOps friendliness.

[09:14] Mei Lin: Finance won’t live in Grafana, realistically.

[09:18] Emily Stone: Correct.

[09:20] Aisha Bello: Ok. I’ll propose: Grafana panels with alert rules, and a Console landing page with the key tiles and drilldowns.

[09:31] Talia Benson: Paging policy: who gets paged?

[09:35] Aisha Bello: If it’s drift beyond paging threshold or freshness breach during close window, it should page Eng on-call (Telemetry/SRE). Finance gets notified in Slack with context.

[09:47] Emily Stone: During close we also need a “stop the line” signal. Like, if data freshness is beyond X hours, we can’t finalize numbers.

[09:57] Rishi Malhotra: What’s a reasonable freshness SLA? Two hours? Six?

[10:02] Caleb Johnson: For the full pipeline, two hours is ambitious. But we can do something like: usage events < 30 min, recon export < 2 hours, ledger postings may be daily.

[10:15] Logan Wright: Yeah ledger is batchy. But for sanity checks, the export table should be near-real-time-ish.

[10:23] Mei Lin: For close, we can accept D+1 completion with a defined cutoff. The important part is we measure late arrivals and have an exception procedure.

[10:37] Talia Benson: Let’s capture: define freshness SLAs per stage, and define the month-end freeze window/cutoff.

[10:46] Emily Stone: And define what happens if a backfill changes a closed month. That needs Finance and Legal sign-off, period.

[10:54] Michael Grant: (faint) I’ll just add: from Legal, we need consistent definitions in terms. If we change usage definition materially, it impacts contracts. So I want to be looped on anything that changes “what is a billable token” or retry semantics.

[11:12] Talia Benson: Thanks Michael. We’ll route any definition changes through Legal review.

[11:17] Nadia Rahman: On retries: we should be explicit. If Redwood retries internally due to transient GPU failure, customer should not be double charged. That’s the core bug class we’re eliminating.

[11:31] Cole Summers: Agree. The trick is when the first attempt partially streamed tokens before failing. We need a rule: do we bill for tokens successfully delivered, or tokens generated, or something else.

[11:46] Nikhil Sharma: Customers expect to be billed for what they got. If they got partial output, they might accept partial charge. But double charge is unacceptable.

[11:56] Emily Stone: And if we credit, we need a policy. Credits need to be documented for audit.

[12:03] Rishi Malhotra: Also, caching. If we serve from cache, cost is lower. We may not want to bill zero, but we need consistent “billed_tokens” definition and disclose it.

[12:16] Nadia Rahman: That’s why I’m advocating for separate fields: raw_tokens (what the model would see), processed_tokens (what we actually ran), billed_tokens (what we bill). Then cache_hit tells you if processed was reduced.

[12:34] Talia Benson: Ok. Next: ownership. Logan, you’re the Eng lead overall?

[12:39] Logan Wright: For telemetry/metering sanity, yes. Nadia is the spec/attribution owner.

[12:45] Talia Benson: Mei for recon datasets and drift queries.

[12:48] Mei Lin: Yep.

[12:50] Talia Benson: Aisha for dashboards/alerts and SLOs.

[12:53] Aisha Bello: Correct.

[12:55] Talia Benson: Emily for close checklist and sign-off policy.

[12:58] Emily Stone: Yep.

[13:00] Talia Benson: Chris for experiment segmentation/caveats template.

[13:03] Chris Osei: Yup.

[13:04] Talia Benson: Connor for change control and rollout gates.

[13:07] Connor O'Brien: Yes.

[13:09] Talia Benson: Leila for tests/perf canary scenarios.

[13:12] Leila Farouk: Yep.

[13:14] Talia Benson: Cole for billing hooks idempotency and dedupe behavior.

[13:17] Cole Summers: Yep.

[13:20] Talia Benson: Great. Let’s talk concrete outputs by end of next week.

[13:25] Nadia Rahman: I can draft KPI definitions and a first pass spec doc: what the fields mean, what the invariants are, and which checks map to which failure mode.

[13:37] Mei Lin: I’ll do the “what tables exist” inventory and propose a single recon export schema. But I need access approvals sometimes.

[13:46] Emily Stone: We can handle access through the normal process, but we need audit logging. Billing ledger is restricted.

[13:53] Caleb Johnson: We can set up a restricted role for Finance and the recon export table can be broader, with PII removed.

[14:03] Talia Benson: Cool. Any disagreement on drift checks being daily and weekly?

[14:08] Logan Wright: Daily for freshness, duplicates, null dims. Drift can be daily with a rolling window, and weekly for deeper slices.

[14:18] Aisha Bello: And we should annotate known incidents. Like, if a backfill is running, the dashboard should show it so the alert doesn’t cause panic.

[14:28] Connor O'Brien: That’s doable—release can send annotations.

[14:33] Chris Osei: For experiments, I’d like an annotation too: “meter v2 rolled out here,” so analysis doesn’t mix.

[14:41] Talia Benson: Great. Let’s do it.

[14:44] (crosstalk)

[14:46] Rishi Malhotra: One more: I want a weekly review cadence. Like 20 minutes, we look at drift and top anomalies.

[14:54] Emily Stone: Yes, especially around close.

[14:57] Logan Wright: Ok. We can do a standing sync.

[15:02] Talia Benson: I’ll set up a weekly “meter sanity review” meeting.

[15:09] Talia Benson: Before we wrap, let’s capture the decisions:
- Ledger is invoicing truth, but must reconcile to usage export within thresholds.
- We will define KPIs: drift, freshness, dupes, null dims, late arrivals.
- Any meter definition change goes through shadow billing compare + Finance sign-off; Legal review if definitions change.
- Experiment analyses must segment pre/post meter changes.

[15:36] Talia Benson: Anything else?

[15:38] Cole Summers: Just to be clear, idempotency key: we should require it for clients eventually, but today we can’t. So we need a server-generated dedupe key too.

[15:49] Nadia Rahman: Yep. We can use request_id + timestamp bucket + account_id, but we need to be careful not to collapse legit distinct requests.

[16:01] Logan Wright: We’ll propose a dedupe window and evaluate false positives.

[16:07] Leila Farouk: For tests, I’ll need the expected behaviors written down. Otherwise the tests are arbitrary.

[16:14] Nadia Rahman: That’ll be in the spec.

[16:17] Talia Benson: Perfect. I’m going to read back action items and then we’ll end.

[16:22] Talia Benson: Nadia: KPI definitions + spec draft by Wed.
Mei: source-of-truth tables and join keys by Thu.
Aisha: alert thresholds + routing proposal by Fri.
Logan: top failure modes list by Wed.
Leila: perf canary scenarios by next week.
Emily: close constraints + freeze window doc by end of week.
Chris: experiment segmentation/caveats template by Fri.
Cole: idempotency key/dedupe behavior writeup by Thu.
Connor: change control checklist by end of week.

[16:55] Talia Benson: Thanks all. I’ll send notes and schedule the follow-up.

(Transcript continues with minor scheduling chatter; recording ends.)

source.summary: Company-wide strategic and operational all-hands. CEO opened with mission and north star, brief finance/usage metrics, product and infra updates, Dedicated/Private go-to-market signals, hiring cadence, and a cross-functional Q&A. Several tactical action items for product, infra, sales, and people ops. Tone: candid, pragmatic; several questions on latency/cost tradeoffs and private deployments. Decks referenced: Q1-ops-deck, roadmap-1p, benchmark-sheets.
topics: Company mission refresh and north star
Monthly metrics: ARR, usage, latency percentiles, cost per token trends
Product: hosting features, rollout and experiments for caching/quantization
Dedicated & Private: onboarding roadmap and compliance updates
Hiring and org priorities (eng, sales, security)
Open Q&A: latency vs cost, SLAs, SOC2/KMS questions
action_items: Priya: Share Q1 product roadmap deck by 2026-02-02
Elliot: Run internal perf benchmark for 8k sequence use-case and upload spreadsheet by 2026-02-09
Samir: Update enterprise GTM one-pager with private deployment pricing and circulate by 2026-02-05
Lina: Publish hiring ramp plan for Eng and Security headcount by 2026-02-12
Ava: Schedule leadership offsite for strategy alignment week of 2026-03-09
next_steps: Share recording and slide deck to #all-hands channel
Follow-up office hours with Product and Infra next Wednesday (calendar invite)
Run two targeted canary rollouts for caching improvements this quarter
Security to publish SOC 2 status doc and KMS integration guide
source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-01-29 10:00 UTC | Duration: ~72 minutes | Attendees: company-wide all-hands (NA/EU/APAC). Recorded by Fireflies.

[00:00] Ava Chen (CEO): Morning everyone, thanks for joining. Uh, quick check, we have people in three regions so I'm going to keep the prelude short. I'm Ava, for folks who are new, I run product and go-to-market strategy as well as the, the big picture. Today is a strategic and tactical briefing — north star, progress against Q1 ops and then a live Q&A. Slides are up in the Q1-ops-deck link in the calendar, and we'll post the recording.

[02:10] Miguel Ortega (CTO): Quick infra note, we've seen steady improvements in  p95 latency after the KV caching change, but there is still tail noise for long context sessions. The optimization path here is twofold: better kernel selection and more aggressive prefix cache eviction policies. We'll be running an 8k seq benchmark next week; Elliot will lead.

[04:30] Priya Kapoor (Head of Product): On product, the roadmap slide (roadmap-1p) shows three feature areas: streaming/tool-calling polish, model catalog trust signals, and the Optimize recommendations product. We're prioritizing quantization profiles per-model so customers can swap to int8 variants without dev friction. There's some ambiguity in the UX for model variant fallback — we want to avoid surprise quality regressions.

[06:00] Elliot Reed (VP Eng): Yeah and implementation detail: the runtime now supports selective kv snapshotting which reduces memory for multi-tenant Dedicated hosts. There's a small risk window with snapshot compaction during heavy GC windows, so we'll do staggered compaction. Also note: K-V cash (transcriber mis-heard) sorry K-V cache, it's still the biggest lever for cost reduction on inference.

[08:45] Ava: Finance quick hit — ARR growth is on track for the quarter, usage ramped 18% month-over-month driven by a few PLG cohort customers. Unit economics: cost per token improved 6% thanks to batching and new kernel picks. Still, Dedicated onboarding is a heavier lift and has different margin profiles.

[11:20] Samir Patel (Head of Sales): On sales: we've converted three mid-market accounts to Dedicated trials this month. The feedback loop: customers ask for a more prescriptive sizing guide and a clear on-prem roadmap. We'll publish a one-pager with example configs. Also many procurement teams ask for SOC 2+ and KMS options — we have to make the materials a front-and-center artifact in the sales deck.

[14:05] Lina Morales (Head of People): Hiring: we are accelerating senior hires in infra and security, and have an apprenticeship program for SWE II-level candidates. The goal is to be talent-ready for Dedicated expansion. We are also tracking onboarding metrics; time-to-productive has improved but still an area to tighten.

[16:40] Audience question (Speaker 6): Hi quick question about private deployment — what does the timeline look like for a full VPC-on-prem pattern?

[16:55] Miguel: Good question, timeline is layered: private control plane MVP by end of Q2, full on-prem package (air-gapped) we estimate late Q3 subject to customer-specific compliance work. The tricky bits are KMS/HSM integration and network NAT traversal for telemetry. We'll publish a compliance checklist.

[19:20] Audience (Speaker 12): Are we offering any migration support or runbook? It's hard for customers to lift-and-shift models.

[19:35] Priya: Yes, migration playbooks will land in the onboarding portal. We'll also offer a 'pilot bundle' that includes bench runs and perf tuning. That's a new SKU idea Samir is exploring.

[21:50] Ava: Quick readout on risk and fallbacks — we need graceful degradation policies so customer-facing apps don't see big quality cliffs when we route to smaller model variants. The router should be policy-driven: latency budget then fallback. We are codifying this in the rollout spec.

[23:30] Elliot: On the runtime side, the router requires metadata tagging on model variants and latency profiles per region. We'll need product to surface model health metrics in the console.

[25:10] Audience (Speaker 9): Is there guidance we can share with customers about latency vs cost tradeoffs? E.g., if I care about p99 under 300ms what's the recommendation?

[25:30] Miguel: We will publish simple profiles: low-latency (dedicated, smaller seq, aggressive batching disabled), balanced (hosted with prefix cache enabled), and low-cost (quantized variants + batching). But these are rules of thumb — encourage customers to run benchmark workloads. We'll provide a bench spreadsheet template (benchmark-sheets).

[28:05] Samir: Sales needs a crisp slide that maps customer personas to recommended deployment modes and an estimated per-1M-token cost at current run rates. That should cut discovery time in half.

[30:40] Audience (Speaker 17): Any updates on SLAs and SLOs for Dedicated customers?

[30:55] Ava: We're drafting an SLA for Dedicated that includes uptime and latency targets. For hosted API we'll keep tiered SLOs. Legal is working on the contract language. We also need a clear incident communication plan for enterprise customers.

[33:15] Miguel: There was a question earlier about auditing and logs — audit logs are already available in the Private control plane, but we need better retention and export UX. KMS key rotation and HSM support are upcoming items.

[35:50] Priya: Product experiments — we will trial two UX variants for model fallback, one that is explicit and one that's automatic with a rollback toggle. We'll measure confusion and support ticket volume during the trials.

[38:10] Lina: People ops note — we plan to run a pulse survey about workload and burnout next week. Please take it, it's anonymous, and results will inform sprint planning.

[40:00] Open Q&A rapid-fire segment.

[40:10] Speaker 19: When are we opening the EU region for dedicated trials?

[40:20] Samir: We are onboarding a partner infra region in Dublin; early access will be limited but we expect more capacity in Q2.

[41:45] Speaker 11: Any changes to pricing or credits for PLG customers?

[41:55] Ava: No immediate price changes; we're experimenting with usage tiers and promotional credits for activation. We'll share a proposal in the finance thread.

[43:30] Technical interjection (Speaker labeled Speaker 3): A note on tooling: telemetry spans sometimes get truncated across the request-router path, which makes tracing hard. Infra is adding more consistent trace IDs to correlate token-level events.

[45:15] Miguel: Another infra note, kernel selection heuristics currently are heuristics based on length distribution, but we want a feedback loop that uses live latency vs cost signals. That requires offline job and an online feature to surface picks.

[47:50] Priya: On Optimize (the recommendations toolkit), early customers show 10-15% unit cost reductions w/o quality regressions when we suggest caching + quantization combos. We need more A/Bs to prove across workloads.

[50:20] Audience: Any notable case studies we can cite?

[50:30] Samir: A PLG customer reduced run cost by 12% after enabling prefix caching — we will anonymize and write a short win to put in the customer notes.

[52:10] Ava: Leadership ask — we need crisp, short comms: 1) what changed, 2) why it matters, 3) what we expect people to do. Use the new comms template. Also the leadership offsite will be a calendar block; RSVP when invite lands.

[54:00] Q&A about support load during canaries.

[54:15] Elliot: We'll run canaries on small cohorts and provide an on-call rotation for the first 72 hours. Infra will instrument rollback health checks.

[56:30] Audience: Are we tracking model quality regressions as a first class metric?

[56:40] Priya: Yes, quality monitoring will link to prompt sets and regression alerts. We will map alerts to a triage process with owners.

[59:00] Short discussion about region routing and data residency.

[60:20] Miguel: Data residency requires both control plane configuration and per-tenant routing rules; Private mode customers get stronger guarantees. We'll document region-aware routing patterns.

[62:45] Lina: Final people update — compensation cycles and salary review are on track, details with managers in the next two weeks.

[64:10] Closing remarks, Ava: Thank you all. Main asks: read the roadmap, participate in the product office hours, and review the hiring plan. We'll follow up with detailed notes, action items and links.

[66:00] Post-meeting logistical notes: recording will be posted, slide deck attached.

Transcription notes: occasional mis-hears (e.g., K-V cash for KV cache, sock two for SOC 2), punctuation run-on sentences and some speaker attribution are approximate.

source.transcript:
Meeting: Hardware tuning profiles pack - weekly sync
Date: 2025-12-03
Start time: 17:00 UTC
Duration: 47 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees (Redwood): Talia Benson, Hiro Tanaka, Sofia Ivanova, Irene Choi, Rina Kobayashi, Claire Dubois, Ethan Park, Benji Okafor, Omar El-Sayed, Kira Volkov, Sean Gallagher, Vanessa Ortiz

Auto-summary (generated)
- H100 preset looks net-positive on throughput with a tail-latency caveat that improved after the fairness patch; need one more matrix run to confirm.
- L40S remains the main stability risk due to long-context OOM; KV-cache budgeting guardrails are close but need validation.
- B200 stays behind experimental flag; limited fleet access + driver/CUDA constraints; need stricter gating and region-level disable.
- Perf-canary gates are noisy; plan to adjust thresholds and use multi-run aggregation.

Topics
1) Milestone check: profile pack v0.3.0 content
2) H100 benchmark deltas (balanced vs throughput-first)
3) L40S OOMs + KV cache guardrails
4) B200 experimental profile status and fleet constraints
5) Override order / loader edge cases (SKU strings, MIG)
6) Perf-canary + rollout guardrails

[00:00] Talia Benson: Alright, we’ll get started. This is the hardware tuning profiles weekly. Goal today: quick status from Kernels, Scheduling, Infra, Bench. Then blockers and next steps. We’re aiming for v0.3 pack content freeze end of week-ish, depending on the last matrix run.

[00:22] Talia Benson: Hiro, can you start with H100 kernel side? What changed since last week.

[00:28] Hiro Tanaka: Yeah. So kernel autoselect heuristics by sequence length buckets are in good shape. We adjusted the cutoffs. Previously we were switching too early and it hurt prefill on the mid bucket—like 2k to 4k tokens. Now it’s more stable.

[00:45] Hiro Tanaka: Also, the “balanced” preset is basically stable, but we still have a couple knobs I’m not sure we should freeze in the profile. Specifically the block size toggle—um the one that picks the fused attention variant. It’s safe on H100 SXM, less sure on PCIe.

[01:03] Irene Choi: And we do have both in fleet. The hosted pools are mostly SXM, dedicated varies.

[01:08] Hiro Tanaka: Exactly. So I want to guard on memory bandwidth / device name.

[01:13] Ethan Park: Just to clarify, guardrails here means the profile includes “applies_if” rules, right? Like “sku_family=h100” plus “form_factor=sxm” or “hbm>=80GB”?

[01:25] Hiro Tanaka: Yeah, that’s the idea.

[01:27] Talia Benson: Okay, so for v0.3 we keep it simple: H100 with explicit differentiation if needed. Ethan, that’s compatible with the schema we landed?

[01:35] Ethan Park: Mostly. We can do it without nesting complex expressions. But we need to keep it readable. Also we have to be careful with string variants. Like “NVIDIA H100 PCIe” vs “H100-80GB” and sometimes the cloud provider adds junk.

[01:49] Irene Choi: Yeah. We’ve got a list. I’ll send the updated list after this.

[01:54] Talia Benson: Great. Sofia, scheduling side?

[01:58] Sofia Ivanova: On scheduler: the big change is we picked up Marcus’s fix for prefill/decode fairness. That was the tail regression we saw when continuous batching was set more aggressively. The patch basically prevents decode starvation when we have a few long prefills.

[02:17] Sofia Ivanova: On the benchmark runs from yesterday, p95 improved. It’s not totally gone in the worst-case streaming scenario, but it’s within the guardrail bands we discussed with SRE.

[02:28] Sean Gallagher: Which band? Like, what are we talking.

[02:31] Sofia Ivanova: For the streaming chat workload at concurrency 64, p95 went from +18% to +6% versus baseline, while tokens/sec stayed +11%. So still a trade, but it’s less scary.

[02:45] Sean Gallagher: Okay. +6% might be acceptable if we’re honest about expected behavior and we can rollback quickly.

[02:52] Talia Benson: Rina/Claire, can you anchor this with the matrix summary? Are those numbers representative or cherry picked.

[02:59] Rina Kobayashi: Um, it’s representative for the “streaming mix” bucket. We have three main mixes right now: non-streaming summarization, streaming chat, and long-context Q&A. The streaming one is the noisy one.

[03:12] Claire Dubois: Also, we still don’t have the “tool calling” pattern in the matrix. Applied ML asked for that because the stop-token and structured output changes the decode pacing. I can add it but we won’t have perfect realism for v0.3.

[03:25] Talia Benson: Understood. Let’s add a lightweight version, not derail.

[03:30] Omar El-Sayed: Can I jump in on “long-context Q&A” because that’s where L40S is exploding.

[03:35] Talia Benson: Yep, go ahead.

[03:38] Omar El-Sayed: So L40S OOMs aren’t just “too big KV”. It’s fragmentation plus some caching behavior. When we do long prompts with moderate concurrency, the KV cache budget expands and we don’t reclaim fast enough. I’ve got a guardrail patch that caps KV per request and shifts to a more conservative eviction.

[03:59] Rina Kobayashi: Is that the thing that was called “KV cash” in the logs? Sorry, I keep reading it like money.

[04:04] Omar El-Sayed: Yeah, the logs are… not great.

[04:08] Benji Okafor: Also the config key name is “kv_cache_budget_mb” and someone’s typo’d it in one of the YAML examples. We should fix docs before it becomes a support issue.

[04:17] Talia Benson: Please capture that as an action item; we can fold into docs PR.

[04:22] Omar El-Sayed: For L40S: with the cap, OOMs went away in my local repro, but p50 got slightly worse on short prompts due to more frequent evictions.

[04:33] Sofia Ivanova: That’s expected. The question is: does it hurt p95 a lot.

[04:38] Rina Kobayashi: We didn’t finish the full L40S matrix because the perf-canary runner died mid run. It’s flaky.

[04:45] Kira Volkov: That’s the same flake I’ve been tracking. The job sometimes pins CPU and the driver resets. It’s not strictly a runtime regression.

[04:54] Vanessa Ortiz: That might be the runner node type. Those L40S boxes have weird CPU steal. We can allocate a different host class.

[05:02] Talia Benson: Okay, but for the pack release we need stable validation. Can we prioritize making L40S runner stable this week.

[05:10] Vanessa Ortiz: Yes, I’ll sync with infra CI folks. We can reserve a pool.

[05:15] Talia Benson: Great.

[05:17] Talia Benson: B200 status. Irene?

[05:20] Irene Choi: Limited access. We have, like, two nodes in us-east and one in eu-west. Driver is the big constraint. Some nodes are on a different branch; CUDA minor version mismatch. So we can’t run the same build everywhere.

[05:36] Hiro Tanaka: Also kernel differences. Some of the H100 heuristics don’t port cleanly. We have to keep the B200 profile experimental and strict-check the driver/CUDA. If check fails, fallback to generic.

[05:50] Ethan Park: The loader supports “requires” with min runtime version and min driver. But we need to decide what we log and what we do when it doesn’t match.

[05:59] Benji Okafor: Current behavior is: if incompatible, log warning, select default profile. But in Private deployments, warnings get ignored. We added startup log matrix in the runtime to show runtime version vs profile version. People will still ignore it.

[06:14] Sean Gallagher: From oncall perspective: I want a big obvious metric: selected_profile, source, and whether we fell back due to compatibility.

[06:22] Kira Volkov: That’s in the metrics PR. It emits “profile_selected{profile=..., source=..., reason=...}” basically.

[06:31] Talia Benson: Okay. For B200, we stick to “off by default” and enable in one pool only. We also need a clear rollback toggle.

[06:41] Irene Choi: Already planning to disable by pool config. But we need override precedence: fleet default vs per-pool vs per-customer.

[06:49] Ethan Park: That’s the ADR. In short: default is in runtime, pack overlays, then fleet/pool overrides, then customer overrides, then per-route overrides. Env var remains highest priority for emergency.

[07:03] Sean Gallagher: Env var at highest priority makes me nervous, but I get why. For emergency we want it.

[07:08] Talia Benson: Let’s park ADR debate; we have to ship.

[07:12] Claire Dubois: Can I ask about “expected behavior” language. Last week we had a docs thread: we need to avoid sounding like we guarantee p95 improves.

[16:35] (The transcript continues; mid-section includes detailed Q&A on perf-canary thresholds, startup logging, and B200 gating checks.)

[24:10] Kira Volkov: One thing: gating on p95 as an absolute number is hard because different runs are noisy. That’s why I’m pushing relative change vs baseline on the same runner.

[24:22] Sean Gallagher: Relative change is fine as long as baseline is recent. Otherwise it drifts.

[24:28] Claire Dubois: We can pin baseline artifacts per runtime release. The matrix runner can store last green baseline.

[24:36] Rina Kobayashi: Yeah, the runner emits a summary JSON artifact. We can compare.

[24:44] Talia Benson: Okay.

[31:05] Irene Choi: For the EU-west B200 node: we already saw weird regressions last time. I’d prefer we disable by default there until we know the driver is consistent.

[31:15] Hiro Tanaka: Agreed.

[31:17] Benji Okafor: That can be a pool override that forces profile “generic-safe”.

[31:24] Sean Gallagher: Perfect.

[38:02] Claire Dubois: Quick doc note: please stop saying “guarantee”. Say “expected”. Also we should include “known failure modes”: long prompt distributions, fragmentation, MIG.

[38:16] Talia Benson: Yes.

[42:40] Talia Benson: Recap time. H100: we’re close, one rerun after fairness fix, likely lock balanced preset. L40S: must fix runner flake and validate KV budget guardrail across long-context bucket. B200: experimental flag, strict version checks, disable in EU-west by default. Perf canary: adjust to median-of-3, tighten non-latency metrics. Runbook: concrete rollback thresholds and toggles.

[43:10] Talia Benson: Did I miss anything.

[43:12] (crosstalk)

[43:13] Sofia Ivanova: No, that’s it.

[43:15] Hiro Tanaka: I’ll share the final bucket mapping options in Slack.

[43:18] Rina Kobayashi: I’ll publish the artifact once H100 rerun finishes.

[43:21] Irene Choi: I’ll send SKU strings and MIG variants.

text:
Meeting: Hardware tuning profiles pack - weekly sync
Date: 2025-12-03
Start time: 17:00 UTC
Duration: 47 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees (Redwood): Talia Benson, Hiro Tanaka, Sofia Ivanova, Irene Choi, Rina Kobayashi, Claire Dubois, Ethan Park, Benji Okafor, Omar El-Sayed, Kira Volkov, Sean Gallagher, Vanessa Ortiz

Auto-summary (generated)
- H100 preset looks net-positive on throughput with a tail-latency caveat that improved after the fairness patch; need one more matrix run to confirm.
- L40S remains the main stability risk due to long-context OOM; KV-cache budgeting guardrails are close but need validation.
- B200 stays behind experimental flag; limited fleet access + driver/CUDA constraints; need stricter gating and region-level disable.
- Perf-canary gates are noisy; plan to adjust thresholds and use multi-run aggregation.

Topics
1) Milestone check: profile pack v0.3.0 content
2) H100 benchmark deltas (balanced vs throughput-first)
3) L40S OOMs + KV cache guardrails
4) B200 experimental profile status and fleet constraints
5) Override order / loader edge cases (SKU strings, MIG)
6) Perf-canary + rollout guardrails

[00:00] Talia Benson: Alright, we’ll get started. This is the hardware tuning profiles weekly. Goal today: quick status from Kernels, Scheduling, Infra, Bench. Then blockers and next steps. We’re aiming for v0.3 pack content freeze end of week-ish, depending on the last matrix run.

[00:22] Talia Benson: Hiro, can you start with H100 kernel side? What changed since last week.

[00:28] Hiro Tanaka: Yeah. So kernel autoselect heuristics by sequence length buckets are in good shape. We adjusted the cutoffs. Previously we were switching too early and it hurt prefill on the mid bucket—like 2k to 4k tokens. Now it’s more stable.

[00:45] Hiro Tanaka: Also, the “balanced” preset is basically stable, but we still have a couple knobs I’m not sure we should freeze in the profile. Specifically the block size toggle—um the one that picks the fused attention variant. It’s safe on H100 SXM, less sure on PCIe.

[01:03] Irene Choi: And we do have both in fleet. The hosted pools are mostly SXM, dedicated varies.

[01:08] Hiro Tanaka: Exactly. So I want to guard on memory bandwidth / device name.

[01:13] Ethan Park: Just to clarify, guardrails here means the profile includes “applies_if” rules, right? Like “sku_family=h100” plus “form_factor=sxm” or “hbm>=80GB”?

[01:25] Hiro Tanaka: Yeah, that’s the idea.

[01:27] Talia Benson: Okay, so for v0.3 we keep it simple: H100 with explicit differentiation if needed. Ethan, that’s compatible with the schema we landed?

[01:35] Ethan Park: Mostly. We can do it without nesting complex expressions. But we need to keep it readable. Also we have to be careful with string variants. Like “NVIDIA H100 PCIe” vs “H100-80GB” and sometimes the cloud provider adds junk.

[01:49] Irene Choi: Yeah. We’ve got a list. I’ll send the updated list after this.

[01:54] Talia Benson: Great. Sofia, scheduling side?

[01:58] Sofia Ivanova: On scheduler: the big change is we picked up Marcus’s fix for prefill/decode fairness. That was the tail regression we saw when continuous batching was set more aggressively. The patch basically prevents decode starvation when we have a few long prefills.

[02:17] Sofia Ivanova: On the benchmark runs from yesterday, p95 improved. It’s not totally gone in the worst-case streaming scenario, but it’s within the guardrail bands we discussed with SRE.

[02:28] Sean Gallagher: Which band? Like, what are we talking.

[02:31] Sofia Ivanova: For the streaming chat workload at concurrency 64, p95 went from +18% to +6% versus baseline, while tokens/sec stayed +11%. So still a trade, but it’s less scary.

[02:45] Sean Gallagher: Okay. +6% might be acceptable if we’re honest about expected behavior and we can rollback quickly.

[02:52] Talia Benson: Rina/Claire, can you anchor this with the matrix summary? Are those numbers representative or cherry picked.

[02:59] Rina Kobayashi: Um, it’s representative for the “streaming mix” bucket. We have three main mixes right now: non-streaming summarization, streaming chat, and long-context Q&A. The streaming one is the noisy one.

[03:12] Claire Dubois: Also, we still don’t have the “tool calling” pattern in the matrix. Applied ML asked for that because the stop-token and structured output changes the decode pacing. I can add it but we won’t have perfect realism for v0.3.

[03:25] Talia Benson: Understood. Let’s add a lightweight version, not derail.

[03:30] Omar El-Sayed: Can I jump in on “long-context Q&A” because that’s where L40S is exploding.

[03:35] Talia Benson: Yep, go ahead.

[03:38] Omar El-Sayed: So L40S OOMs aren’t just “too big KV”. It’s fragmentation plus some caching behavior. When we do long prompts with moderate concurrency, the KV cache budget expands and we don’t reclaim fast enough. I’ve got a guardrail patch that caps KV per request and shifts to a more conservative eviction.

[03:59] Rina Kobayashi: Is that the thing that was called “KV cash” in the logs? Sorry, I keep reading it like money.

[04:04] Omar El-Sayed: Yeah, the logs are… not great.

[04:08] Benji Okafor: Also the config key name is “kv_cache_budget_mb” and someone’s typo’d it in one of the YAML examples. We should fix docs before it becomes a support issue.

[04:17] Talia Benson: Please capture that as an action item; we can fold into docs PR.

[04:22] Omar El-Sayed: For L40S: with the cap, OOMs went away in my local repro, but p50 got slightly worse on short prompts due to more frequent evictions.

[04:33] Sofia Ivanova: That’s expected. The question is: does it hurt p95 a lot.

[04:38] Rina Kobayashi: We didn’t finish the full L40S matrix because the perf-canary runner died mid run. It’s flaky.

[04:45] Kira Volkov: That’s the same flake I’ve been tracking. The job sometimes pins CPU and the driver resets. It’s not strictly a runtime regression.

[04:54] Vanessa Ortiz: That might be the runner node type. Those L40S boxes have weird CPU steal. We can allocate a different host class.

[05:02] Talia Benson: Okay, but for the pack release we need stable validation. Can we prioritize making L40S runner stable this week.

[05:10] Vanessa Ortiz: Yes, I’ll sync with infra CI folks. We can reserve a pool.

[05:15] Talia Benson: Great.

[05:17] Talia Benson: B200 status. Irene?

[05:20] Irene Choi: Limited access. We have, like, two nodes in us-east and one in eu-west. Driver is the big constraint. Some nodes are on a different branch; CUDA minor version mismatch. So we can’t run the same build everywhere.

[05:36] Hiro Tanaka: Also kernel differences. Some of the H100 heuristics don’t port cleanly. We have to keep the B200 profile experimental and strict-check the driver/CUDA. If check fails, fallback to generic.

[05:50] Ethan Park: The loader supports “requires” with min runtime version and min driver. But we need to decide what we log and what we do when it doesn’t match.

[05:59] Benji Okafor: Current behavior is: if incompatible, log warning, select default profile. But in Private deployments, warnings get ignored. We added startup log matrix in the runtime to show runtime version vs profile version. People will still ignore it.

[06:14] Sean Gallagher: From oncall perspective: I want a big obvious metric: selected_profile, source, and whether we fell back due to compatibility.

[06:22] Kira Volkov: That’s in the metrics PR. It emits “profile_selected{profile=..., source=..., reason=...}” basically.

[06:31] Talia Benson: Okay. For B200, we stick to “off by default” and enable in one pool only. We also need a clear rollback toggle.

[06:41] Irene Choi: Already planning to disable by pool config. But we need override precedence: fleet default vs per-pool vs per-customer.

[06:49] Ethan Park: That’s the ADR. In short: default is in runtime, pack overlays, then fleet/pool overrides, then customer overrides, then per-route overrides. Env var remains highest priority for emergency.

[07:03] Sean Gallagher: Env var at highest priority makes me nervous, but I get why. For emergency we want it.

[07:08] Talia Benson: Let’s park ADR debate; we have to ship.

[07:12] Claire Dubois: Can I ask about “expected behavior” language. Last week we had a docs thread: we need to avoid sounding like we guarantee p95 improves.

[07:20] Talia Benson: Correct. We say “expected behavior under this workload envelope”. We explicitly call out tradeoffs and failure modes.

[07:28] Sofia Ivanova: I’ll add a line: throughput-first can increase tail latency, especially streaming and long-prefill mixes. Balanced mode is default for Hosted. Throughput-first maybe for Dedicated batchy workloads.

[07:42] Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also some kernels are better at long seq but worse at short seq. That’s why the bucketization exists.

[07:51] Rina Kobayashi: On H100 matrix: overall tokens/sec improved 8–15% depending on model family. p50 latency improved slightly on non-streaming. p95 on streaming is variable but less bad after fairness fix.

[08:04] Claire Dubois: Which models are in the matrix again? For notes.

[08:08] Rina Kobayashi: Three families: Llama-style 70B-ish, a smaller 8B-ish, and an embedding model. I’m not saying names because we keep swapping the exact ones, but it’s consistent.

[08:20] Talia Benson: Good. Put the exact model pins in the benchmark artifact, not in this call.

[08:25] Kira Volkov: Perf-canary gates: right now thresholds are too tight for p95 on streaming. We’re failing builds due to noise. I propose we run each scenario three times and gate on median. Also widen band for the known noisy buckets.

[08:41] Sean Gallagher: Please run that by SRE. We can’t widen too much and miss regressions.

[08:47] Kira Volkov: Yeah. We can keep strict bands on error rate, OOM, and queue depth. p95 can be median-of-3 with narrower band.

[08:57] Benji Okafor: On queue depth: the new backpressure settings can change queue behavior. It might look “worse” but it’s actually protecting latency. We need to define the metric expectation.

[09:08] Sofia Ivanova: Exactly. A slightly higher queue depth might be fine if tail latency is stable. But if queue grows without bound, that’s bad.

[09:17] Talia Benson: We need a consistent oncall triage list: which dashboards, which metric thresholds mean rollback.

[09:23] Sean Gallagher: For rollback criteria, I want: p95 latency +10% sustained 15 minutes on top routes OR error rate above X OR OOM events > 0 in that pool post-deploy. Plus a “customer ticket spike” clause.

[09:40] Talia Benson: Great. Capture in runbook.

[09:43] (crosstalk)

[09:44] Vanessa Ortiz: Also autoscaling. If we change batching, throughput changes, autoscaling may react weirdly. We should watch utilization and scale oscillations.

[09:54] Irene Choi: Yep. Some pools have different scaling policies. We’ll do staged rollout: one canary pool per region, then 10%, then 50%, then 100.

[10:06] Talia Benson: Okay, let’s get more detailed on H100: balanced vs throughput-first. Sofia, what knobs differ.

[10:13] Sofia Ivanova: Balanced: lower max batch tokens, more strict prefill/decode fairness, smaller queue limits. Throughput-first: higher max batch tokens, more aggressive continuous batching window, less strict fairness.

[10:28] Hiro Tanaka: And kernel side: throughput-first allows selecting kernels that are slightly worse for short sequences but better for long, to maximize aggregate tokens/sec.

[10:40] Claire Dubois: That aligns with workloads: summarization batch jobs vs chat.

[10:45] Talia Benson: So default: Balanced for Hosted. For Dedicated, we can let customers pick. For Private, default Balanced but document override.

[10:56] Benji Okafor: On Private override: Helm values wire in the profile pack and also allow overriding per deployment. We should include safe “known good” examples.

[11:07] Ethan Park: And validation: if someone sets nonsense values, config validation should fail fast.

[11:13] Sean Gallagher: Fail fast is good, but in production it might brick a rollout.

[11:18] Ethan Park: Right. We only fail fast for invalid types/out-of-range. For optional keys, we ignore unknown keys with a warning. That was the compromise.

[11:30] Talia Benson: Okay.

[11:32] Talia Benson: Let’s check blockers. Rina: what’s blocking the next benchmark report.

[11:37] Rina Kobayashi: Two things: L40S runner flake and missing one H100 rerun after the fairness fix merged. The H100 rerun should finish today if the queue cooperates.

[11:49] Kira Volkov: The H100 queue is fine. L40S is the one with, like, random driver resets.

[11:55] Vanessa Ortiz: I’ll get you a reserved node class by tomorrow.

[12:00] Rina Kobayashi: Great.

[12:02] Talia Benson: Irene: any infra blockers.

[12:05] Irene Choi: Only B200 access. We can’t do a full matrix. I can get NVIDIA to lend us more for a week, but not guaranteed.

[12:14] Hiro Tanaka: For v0.3, maybe it’s fine if B200 remains “experimental preview” with limited validation.

[12:22] Talia Benson: That’s consistent with leadership: experimental, gated, clear fallback. No broad enablement.

[12:30] Claire Dubois: Can we ensure docs say “B200 profile requires driver X / CUDA Y” and “not supported in all regions”.

[12:38] Ethan Park: Yes, and runtime logs show compatibility matrix.

[12:42] Benji Okafor: Plus a debug endpoint to dump effective runtime config. Oncall can ask a customer to run it in Private.

[12:50] Sean Gallagher: That’s huge. As long as it’s gated/secured.

[12:54] Benji Okafor: It’s internal-only by default; for Private we require an explicit enable flag and auth.

[13:01] Talia Benson: Cool.

[13:03] (brief pause)

[13:05] Talia Benson: Let’s talk about override precedence edge cases: MIG and multi-GPU nodes. Irene, did we decide per-device vs per-node.

[13:14] Irene Choi: Most of our serving nodes are single GPU, but some Dedicated has multi. MIG is rare but exists.

[13:22] Ethan Park: The current loader selects a profile per process based on detected GPU 0. If you have mixed GPUs, that’s a problem. But we don’t support mixed in one node anyway.

[13:33] Sean Gallagher: What about MIG slices. The device name can include “MIG 1g.10gb” etc.

[13:40] Irene Choi: Yep, that breaks naive string matching. I’ll include the variants we saw.

[13:46] Hiro Tanaka: Kernel presets on MIG are risky. We should treat MIG as “unknown” and choose conservative defaults.

[13:54] Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling also changes; smaller memory means smaller KV budgets. So yes, conservative.

[14:00] Talia Benson: Okay, we’ll add guardrail: if MIG detected, don’t apply tuned profile unless explicitly allowed.

[14:08] Ethan Park: That’s easy.

[14:10] Talia Benson: Now canary rollout plan. Sean, what do you need.

[14:15] Sean Gallagher: I need the runbook to be explicit: which toggle disables profiles for a pool, and where to look. Dashboard should have panels segmented by selected profile. And alert thresholds should be enumerated.

[14:30] Kira Volkov: Observability pack has the new panels. We can link them.

[14:34] Sean Gallagher: Also: who’s on point during rollout windows. I don’t want a Friday night surprise.

[14:40] Talia Benson: We’ll coordinate with release train owner but for now: staged in business hours. Irene, can we commit to that.

[14:47] Irene Choi: Yes.

[14:49] Talia Benson: Great.

[14:51] (transcription note: multiple speakers)

[14:52] Sofia Ivanova: One more thing: the throughput-first preset can cause token latency spikes if the request mix has lots of short prompts. We should keep it opt-in.

[15:02] Hiro Tanaka: Yep.

[15:04] Claire Dubois: That’s also about workload envelope. We should put a table: recommended for “batchy summarization, non-streaming”, not recommended for “interactive streaming chat with tight p95”.

[15:15] Talia Benson: Good, docs will include.

[15:18] Talia Benson: Anything else from Bench.

[15:21] Rina Kobayashi: I want to mention one thing: embedding workloads. The tuned profiles didn’t help much; maybe 1–2% variance. But they didn’t hurt. So we can say “no meaningful change expected.”

[15:33] Talia Benson: Great.

[15:35] Omar El-Sayed: On L40S: we should define safe-by-default memory behavior. Like KV budget is a function of free memory. But we can cap at X. Right now I used 65% of available memory, leaving headroom.

[15:48] Sean Gallagher: Headroom is good. OOMs are the worst.

[15:52] Sofia Ivanova: But if headroom is too big, throughput suffers.

[15:56] Omar El-Sayed: Yep. That’s the trade.

[15:59] Talia Benson: For v0.3: stability > max throughput on L40S.

[16:05] Hiro Tanaka: Agree.

[16:07] Talia Benson: Okay, quick pass on timeline. Today: H100 rerun finishing. Tomorrow: Irene sends SKU variants + reserved L40S runner plan. End of week: v0.3 content freeze pending L40S validation.

[16:23] Benji Okafor: And integration tests for override precedence are in review. I’ll push to merge by Friday.

[16:30] Ethan Park: Schema validation is already merged.

[16:33] Talia Benson: Great.

[16:35] (The transcript continues; mid-section includes detailed Q&A on perf-canary thresholds, startup logging, and B200 gating checks.)

[24:10] Kira Volkov: One thing: gating on p95 as an absolute number is hard because different runs are noisy. That’s why I’m pushing relative change vs baseline on the same runner.

[24:22] Sean Gallagher: Relative change is fine as long as baseline is recent. Otherwise it drifts.

[24:28] Claire Dubois: We can pin baseline artifacts per runtime release. The matrix runner can store last green baseline.

[24:36] Rina Kobayashi: Yeah, the runner emits a summary JSON artifact. We can compare.

[24:44] Talia Benson: Okay.

[31:05] Irene Choi: For the EU-west B200 node: we already saw weird regressions last time. I’d prefer we disable by default there until we know the driver is consistent.

[31:15] Hiro Tanaka: Agreed.

[31:17] Benji Okafor: That can be a pool override that forces profile “generic-safe”.

[31:24] Sean Gallagher: Perfect.

[38:02] Claire Dubois: Quick doc note: please stop saying “guarantee”. Say “expected”. Also we should include “known failure modes”: long prompt distributions, fragmentation, MIG.

[38:16] Talia Benson: Yes.

[42:40] Talia Benson: Recap time. H100: we’re close, one rerun after fairness fix, likely lock balanced preset. L40S: must fix runner flake and validate KV budget guardrail across long-context bucket. B200: experimental flag, strict version checks, disable in EU-west by default. Perf canary: adjust to median-of-3, tighten non-latency metrics. Runbook: concrete rollback thresholds and toggles.

[43:10] Talia Benson: Did I miss anything.

[43:12] (crosstalk)

[43:13] Sofia Ivanova: No, that’s it.

[43:15] Hiro Tanaka: I’ll share the final bucket mapping options in Slack.

[43:18] Rina Kobayashi: I’ll publish the artifact once H100 rerun finishes.

[43:21] Irene Choi: I’ll send SKU strings and MIG variants.

[43:25] Sean Gallagher: Please add rollback criteria draft in the runbook doc by Friday.

[43:32] Talia Benson: Done. Thanks everyone.

End of transcript

source.transcript:
Header:
Date: 2025-09-10
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~75 minutes
Attendees: Sanjay Rao, Leah Kim, Jonah Miles, Ava Chen, Marco Diaz, Priya Singh + all company (townhall)

Summary (auto-gen, may be imperfect):
- Leadership reviewed Q3 performance, runway and hiring pause lift plan.
- Product walked through prioritization of model hosting work, Dedicated capacity optimizations, and private deployment milestones.
- Ops/infra talked about latency SLOs, KV cache improvements, and a migration plan for a critical kernel selector that will reduce 95p latency.
- Low-level Q&A included questions on SOC two, VPC options, on-prem packaging, and hiring priorities.

00:00 Sanjay (CEO): Hey everyone, good afternoon and thanks for joining, um, our autumn townhall. I'm gonna kick it off with a quick update on numbers and then pass to Leah and Jonah for product and infra. We wanted to—uh—make this a bit of an ops deep dive and keep a long Q and A at the end so grab coffee.

00:34 Sanjay: Quick bullets — revenue up 14% quarter-over-quarter, usage growth in hosted API about 20%, churn stable, enterprise pipeline healthy. We did see some bookings push into next quarter on a couple of big deals which means we have to be careful about runway math, but the burn is trending better thanks to cost optimizations.

01:02 Sanjay: Hiring pause is being lifted for priority roles — we will still be deliberate. Priya will cover the hiring cadence shortly. Um, thanks to Eng and Product for hitting a good set of deliveries this quarter.

01:25 Leah (CTO): Hi folks. Quick engineering update — we shipped the first pass of the quantized kernel selector for Llama variants last month. Early internal numbers show about 22% reduction in token cost on our dedicated fleet when using the new path, and it shrinks 95p latency by around 8 to 12 percent. Caveat: measurement noise and the usual, um, benchmark drift.

02:00 Leah: We're also changing how the runtime does prefix caching — there's an experimental feature we call "prefix-K" in the branch, which is intended to reduce repeated prefix compute for multi-turn chat. It's still noisy; we need more telemetry. Side note: some of the internal logs mis-typed it once as "KV cash" in last week's deploy notes, so ignore that in dashboards.

02:28 Jonah (VP Product): Thanks Leah. Product priorities for the next 8–12 weeks — (1) stabilize Dedicated autoscaler and make burst capacity configurable per-tenant, (2) ship private on-prem packaging with a hardened control plane option, (3) make Optimize suggestions available in the Console as a first-class tab. The rationale: customers keep telling us they need predictable throughput and easier ops for on-prem.

03:05 Jonah: About rollouts — we want to give teams the ability to pin model variants per-route and set graceful fallbacks; that'll be a canary + auto-fallback pattern. Goal is to avoid noisy neighbor issues and avoid surprises on latency SLOs.

03:46 Ava (Head Eng): A couple of infra things — we're planning a rolling upgrade of CUDA on the Dedicated fleet in mid-October. We'll do it region by region, low-traffic windows, and there will be a short capacity dip that Marco's team will coordinate with the sales ops. Also, there is a plan to add per-tenant KV cache metrics to the Console so customers can tune cache TTLs.

04:22 Ava: Note — the running test for the new kernel path showed an edge case for seq length = 4096 on some older drivers, it causes a small memory regression; we have a mitigation but it needs more validation.

04:46 Marco (Head Sales): From GTM — enterprise interest in Dedicated and Private is increasing. We closed two pilots for regulated industries with dedicated reservations. Pricing feedback: customers want clearer tradeoffs between quantization, throughput and observed hallucination rates; the word I keep hearing is "predictable." We'll share an updated pilot pack.

05:12 Priya (Head of People): Quick update — we lifted hiring pause selectively for SWE II and above, SRE and security hires prioritized, and we'll run two all-hands hiring Q&As next Tuesday and Thursday for interviewers. Also reminder: we are rolling out updated parental leave policy next month.

05:40 Sanjay: Alright, I'm gonna switch gears to roadmap context and the bets we're making. We've had a strategic thesis for a while: the advantage moves to serving. That hasn't changed. We're doubling down on making inference predictable and cost-efficient across hosted, dedicated, and private. This quarter that means: autoscaling, KV prefix caching improvements, and the private control plane hardening.

06:18 Sanjay: We also want to be disciplined about scope. Some feature asks are delightful but don't move the needle on unit economics or reliability. We'll prioritize accordingly.

07:00 Audience Q&A (open mic)

07:12 Employee 1 (Design): Hi, quick question on the private on-prem timeline — is the initial on-prem deliverable gonna be air-gapped ready or a follow-up?

07:22 Jonah: Short term it's a private VPC control plane with an on-prem optional data plane — air-gap is a follow-up. We want a safe, manageable first release; air-gapped customers have additional compliance glue and that's a second milestone.

07:40 Employee 2 (SWE): Are we planning to support specialized tensor cores for some of the newer chips?

07:48 Leah: Yes, kernel selection will include hardware-specific kernels for tensor cores. That work is in progress; timeline depends on upstream vendor drivers but it's in our internal backlog. Expect a preview in the next quarter.

08:12 Employee 3 (Ops): Any change to SLOs during CUDA upgrades?

08:20 Ava: We'll maintain our public SLOs and communicate windows; the only impact is temporary capacity reduction during each region's brief maintenance window. We'll publish a schedule.

08:48 Employee 4 (Security): Status on SOC two and ISO? We had a customer ask about continuous audit logs and KMS integration.

09:00 Leah: SOC two Type II is in progress; audit readiness checklist nearly complete. KMS integrations for Private are prioritized — HSM/KMS support is in the roadmap and we expect initial partners for cloud KMS by Q4. Note: sometimes the compliance docs auto-generated reports refer to "SOC two" in prose which is odd but it's the same thing.

09:32 Employee 5 (Sales Engineer): Any guidance for customers asking about per-token cost measurement and variances across models?

09:45 Marco: We recommend sharing per-route token breakdowns from the token-level cost dashboard. Also, Recommend using Optimize suggestions (when available) to tune batch sizes and cache TTLs. We'll create a small playbook and sample spreadsheet to share.

10:16 Sanjay: Okay, a couple of specific operational asks — (1) we must reduce our public infra spend by 6% without impacting reliability; (2) ship the private control plane MVP by end of Q4 with the on-prem data plane alpha. Those are our top company-level deliverables this quarter.

11:00 Jonah: To hit those we'll be prioritizing model families that give the best throughput/cost tradeoffs for our largest customers. We'll pause some smaller exploratory integrations to free engineering cycles.
