source.summary: Internal go/no-go for enabling Dedicated staged rollouts for a limited beta (1–2 design partners). Reviewed early beta failures (429 spike due to canary partition starving effective capacity, gate evidence missing due to telemetry tag mismatch, and metrics cardinality regression). Aligning on ship criteria: feature flag default OFF, validated rollback/abort semantics, stable gate evaluation under telemetry lag, and audit export readiness. Decision: GO for limited beta with guardrails and explicit hold/force-promote controls restricted to admin roles; no broad enablement until SIEM export and small-pool safeguards are verified.
topics: Beta entry criteria and enablement scope (account/pool/region flags)
Incident review: canary stage partition led to elevated 429s and queue depth
Gate evaluation behavior under missing metrics / telemetry lag (fail-closed + override)
Launch checklist: migrations/backfill, dashboards/alerts, runbooks, QA
Security/compliance: audit events and export pipeline coverage
questions: Do we block promotion if metrics are stale/unknown, or allow with warning + manual confirmation?
What’s the minimum pool size where stage partitioning is allowed (replica-based vs percentage)?
If a region is impaired mid-stage, do we hold, auto-abort, or shift traffic cross-region for Dedicated pools?
What audit events must be present in export before inviting regulated design partner?
source.transcript:
Meeting Header
Date: 2025-03-12
Time: 10:02 AM PT
Duration: 58 min
Title: Go/No-Go: Dedicated staged rollouts (beta) + incident review + launch checklist
Attendees: Talia Benson, Priya Natarajan, Connor O'Brien, Rafael Mendes, Lauren Bishop, Marissa Cole, Ethan Park, Mina Haddad, Logan Wright, Irene Choi, Kaitlyn Nguyen, Leila Farouk, Allison Grant


00:00:03 Talia Benson: Alright, we’ll get started. This is the go/no-go for the Dedicated staged rollouts beta. Goal today is decision plus any blocking items for the checklist. I’ll keep us tight. Priya, do you want to frame what “beta” means here?

00:00:19 Priya Natarajan: Yeah. Beta here is not broad. It’s one to two design partners, Dedicated pools only, feature flag scoped by account and pool. We’re not turning on arbitrary stages for everyone. We’re validating: stage promotion, gate evaluation, abort/rollback semantics, and that we don’t hurt SLOs.

00:00:41 Lauren Bishop: And to be explicit, the customer expectation is “safer upgrades” not “a general experiment system.” They want 1% then 10% etc, and clear stop-the-line if things go sideways.

00:00:55 Marissa Cole: Console perspective: they also want to see why the gate failed. Like not just “failed,” but what signal, what window, what threshold, and ideally a link to a graph.

00:01:09 Talia Benson: Great. Connor, can you run the launch checklist status? Then we’ll do incident review.

00:01:15 Connor O'Brien: Sure. Checklist items: feature flag is implemented, default OFF. Migration for the rollout plan model is in staging; backfill is behind a job flag. Console endpoints for timeline and gate evidence are in the beta branch. Dashboards PR is merged but we need to confirm it’s using the post-hotfix label set. Runbook exists but Rafael wants to tweak the oncall steps. Biggest gap is audit export—INT ticket says new event types weren’t showing up in the export pipeline.

00:01:48 Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yeah, that’s the one that makes me nervous for the regulated partner. We can create audit events, but if they can’t export to SIEM, they’ll call it non-compliant.

00:01:59 Connor O'Brien: Totally. We can still beta with the startup partner first, but we should gate the bank-y one on export being fixed.

00:02:07 Talia Benson: Okay. Let’s switch to incident review. Rafael, can you summarize the 429 spike situation?

00:02:14 Rafael Mendes: Yep. This was during the early canary stage test. We set stage to “1%” but because the pool is small and the partition ended up effectively being one GPU slice, it reduced usable concurrency. Queue depth spiked, then the request shed logic started returning 429s. It looked like an outage to the customer even though overall fleet was healthy.

00:02:41 Mina Haddad: The routing side picked the stage partition deterministically, but the partition had less headroom than expected. The percent language is misleading when you’re under… like, under a certain replica count.

00:02:54 Irene Choi: Right. In a 16-GPU reservation, “1%” isn’t a thing. You get at least one node or one replica group. So you’re at 6% or whatever the chunk is. And worse, you can’t always spare that node in every region.

00:03:10 Lauren Bishop: This is exactly what customers are afraid of. They try to be safe and we accidentally make it less safe.

00:03:17 Priya Natarajan: So what’s the mitigation? We can’t ship a staged rollout feature that causes a 429 incident by default.

00:03:25 Mina Haddad: Mitigation is guardrails plus replica-based partitions for small pools. The ADR basically says: if pool size below threshold, stages are expressed in “N replicas” not percent. Also we enforce minimum headroom—like you cannot create a canary stage that leaves the baseline partition below X.

00:03:47 Irene Choi: And we need a validation rule: if you don’t have enough capacity per region to create the stage partitions, we reject or force “single region only.” Customers do multi-region Dedicated but often it’s uneven.

00:04:02 Ethan Park: Control plane can validate at create-time with a capacity snapshot, but snapshots can drift. So we also need runtime checks: if a region cannot satisfy the stage allocation, we either hold or fail the promotion.

00:04:17 Rafael Mendes: For SRE, we want: do not proceed if capacity is constrained. That’s a gate. It can be a “saturation/headroom” gate. But also, we need the abort semantics to be clean.

00:04:29 Talia Benson: Let’s capture the open question: region impairment mid-stage. Mina, what happens today?

00:04:35 Mina Haddad: Today, traffic splitting is applied per pool per region. If region B is unhealthy, our global routing can shift to region A depending on the pool policy, but Dedicated customers often pin to region for latency. In a staged rollout, if stage partition in region B is unhealthy, you can get uneven exposure.

00:04:55 Priya Natarajan: That’s… bad. We can’t have “10% overall” becoming “30% in one region” without telling anyone.

00:05:03 Mina Haddad: Agree. The v1 behavior we implemented is “stage applies per region,” and UI needs to show per-region stage health. Promotion gate should require all active regions pass, unless operator explicitly overrides and documents why.

00:05:23 Marissa Cole: In Console we can show a stage as “partial” with region chips. But we need clear copy. Otherwise customer admin will be like “why does it say 10% but only us-east is on it.”

00:05:37 Allison Grant: Observability wise, that means stage_id and region labels are required on the key metrics. Which we had, then we had the cardinality blow-up.

00:05:47 Talia Benson: Let’s talk about the cardinality regression. Logan?

00:05:52 Logan Wright: Yeah. When we first tagged stage_id into request metrics and traces, we accidentally included the raw rollout_plan_id and an internal partition key in a couple places. That created… a ton of series. We hotfixed by allowlisting only stage_number, pool_id hash, and region, and sampling some high-volume spans.

00:06:17 Allison Grant: And we updated the dashboard queries, but we need to confirm nobody is still using the old label set.

00:06:24 Logan Wright: Correct. Also, gate evaluation reads from the aggregated metrics. If telemetry lags, the gate shows “unknown.” That’s where the fail-open vs fail-closed argument came up.

00:06:40 Priya Natarajan: Okay, let’s settle that again. What are we doing if metrics are missing?

00:06:45 Rafael Mendes: SRE position is fail-closed by default. If you can’t prove it’s safe, you don’t promote. But you can force-promote with admin permission and it must be audited with a reason.

00:06:57 Kaitlyn Nguyen: Security is aligned with that, as long as force-promote is RBAC restricted and the audit event has actor, role, and override_reason. And we should surface that in Console in a scary way.

00:07:13 Marissa Cole: “In a scary way” is my favorite spec. But yeah, we can put a confirmation modal that requires typing a reason.

00:07:20 Ethan Park: Control plane already takes “reason” on promote/abort in the API. For force-promote we can require it non-empty.

00:07:29 Connor O'Brien: We also need idempotency keys for promote so retries don’t double-advance stages. I think that’s in.

00:07:37 Ethan Park: It’s in. Promote is idempotent with a key + optimistic concurrency on the rollout state.

00:07:44 Talia Benson: Great. Next early failure: Console gate evidence panel empty. Marissa, what happened?

00:07:52 Marissa Cole: The gate evidence endpoint was querying by stage_id but telemetry tags used stage_number; mismatch. So the panel showed “no data” even though graphs existed. It’s fixed in the latest backend build, but I want QA to rerun.

00:08:09 Leila Farouk: We have a test that asserts evidence payload includes observed_value and threshold for each gate. The flaky part is when metrics are delayed and you get nulls. I can rerun with simulated lag.

00:08:24 Logan Wright: Yeah we should simulate lag. In prod we sometimes have 2-3 minutes of delay, and in an incident it’s worse.

00:08:33 Rafael Mendes: If lag is worse, it should show “stale” not just “fail.” Otherwise operators freak out and abort unnecessarily.

00:08:42 Allison Grant: We can implement staleness detection: if the latest sample timestamp older than, say, 2x window, gate state = UNKNOWN(STALE). That’s different from FAIL.

00:08:56 Priya Natarajan: Good. For beta, is that required?

00:09:00 Logan Wright: I think at least the UI messaging is required. Full staleness detection might be a quick patch but not sure.

00:09:10 Connor O'Brien: From release standpoint, we can accept “unknown” as long as playbook says hold and investigate. But we need consistent behavior.

00:09:19 Talia Benson: Let’s park as action item: Logan + Allison propose the staleness logic and copy.

00:09:27 Talia Benson: Now, decision time. Are there any hard blockers to beta with the startup design partner? Lauren, what’s your view?

00:09:36 Lauren Bishop: I think we can go, but only with the guardrails. Specifically: default stages should be 10/50/100 for small pools, not 1%. Or if we keep 1%, we need the system to translate to “minimum 1 replica” and warn. And we should have hold/abort easily accessible.

00:09:59 Mina Haddad: I’d prefer we keep 1% available only when pool size supports it. Otherwise it’s replica mode.

00:10:07 Irene Choi: Yes, and we set a minimum baseline headroom. Like, don’t let baseline drop under N replicas per region.

00:10:16 Ethan Park: The create validation can enforce that. There’s a question of “what is N.”

00:10:22 Rafael Mendes: For SRE, N depends on the customer SLO and their peak. But we can pick a conservative default: minimum 2 replicas baseline per region, plus keep queue depth under threshold.

00:10:37 Priya Natarajan: Conservative defaults for beta are fine. If customer wants more aggressive, they can request override with SRE approval.

00:10:46 Kaitlyn Nguyen: If we do that, we should log the override as well. “Plan validation overridden,” etc.

00:10:54 Connor O'Brien: Also reminder: audit export pipeline missing events. That doesn’t block startup partner, but blocks regulated. We should decide if regulated partner is in beta tranche one or two.

00:11:08 Lauren Bishop: I recommend tranche one is the startup + maybe EmberSearch-type workload. The bank partner is tranche two after export is fixed.

00:11:18 Talia Benson: Okay, sounds aligned. Let’s do quick round: Go/No-Go for limited beta, and conditions.

00:11:26 Priya Natarajan: Go, with: fail-closed on missing metrics, force-promote admin-only, guardrails for small pools, and rollback tested.

00:11:37 Rafael Mendes: Go, with: oncall playbook updated, alerting routes correct, and we page only on repeated gate failures, not first failure.

00:11:48 Connor O'Brien: Go, with: migration + backfill rehearsed, feature flag scoped, and a documented disable plan.

00:11:56 Marissa Cole: Go, with: gate evidence works in Console and shows “unknown vs fail” clearly, plus audit trail view at least for operators.

00:12:07 Mina Haddad: Go, with: routing behavior documented for rollback and streaming, and region-level representation in Console.

00:12:15 Logan Wright: Go, with: stage-tag allowlist enforced and dashboards verified post-cardinality hotfix.

00:12:24 Irene Choi: Go, with: partitioning guidelines shared with the beta TAM and customer; we cannot do “1% canary” on tiny pools.

00:12:33 Kaitlyn Nguyen: Go, with: RBAC reviewed, audit events include reason fields, and we don’t invite regulated partner until export fix.

00:12:43 Leila Farouk: Go, with: E2E tests pass and we can simulate gate failures without flaking.

00:12:50 Allison Grant: Go, with: dashboard templates not exploding metrics, and alert noise bounded.

00:12:56 Talia Benson: Great. Decision is GO for limited beta with conditions. Now, let’s spend the rest of the time on checklist items and owners.

00:13:06 Connor O'Brien: On flags: we’ll have something like DEDICATED_STAGED_ROLLOUTS_ENABLED, scoped by account_id and pool_id. Also a region-scope guard: we do not allow enabling only one region of a multi-region pool in v1 unless explicitly configured.

00:13:26 Mina Haddad: Thank you. Partial region enablement is a footgun.

00:13:32 Ethan Park: On migrations: rollout plan model has new tables; we can ship migration independent of feature flag. Backfill populates defaults for existing pools but only when feature is enabled.

00:13:48 Connor O'Brien: Right. And rollback plan: if we need to disable, we flip flag off and the routing layer ignores stage partitions, defaults to stable config. But we must ensure no orphaned partitions cause capacity waste.

00:14:05 Irene Choi: Capacity waste is real. If partitions get stuck, we need a cleanup job or manual procedure.

00:14:13 Rafael Mendes: That should be in the runbook: “rollout stuck in held state, partitions remain allocated” -> steps to release.

00:14:22 Marissa Cole: Console should show “held” clearly. Also, do we auto-timeout holds?

00:14:29 Priya Natarajan: Not in beta. Auto-timeout can be dangerous. But maybe we warn after 24 hours.

00:14:37 Ethan Park: We can implement a “hold_expires_at” later. For now, held is sticky until resume/abort.

00:14:48 Talia Benson: Cool. Gate thresholds: are we aligned on defaults? Rafael, any last concerns?

00:14:55 Rafael Mendes: Defaults should align with SLO. For beta: error rate gate maybe 2x baseline or absolute threshold like >1% for 5 minutes, latency p99 not exceeding baseline + say 20% (but baseline calculation must be stable), and saturation headroom must remain >15% GPU util headroom. Also a minimum sample size.

00:15:22 Logan Wright: Sample size is key. If you have 20 requests total, p99 is nonsense.

00:15:28 Allison Grant: We can do “min 500 requests OR min 5 minutes” before gate evaluation is considered valid.

00:15:38 Mina Haddad: But some Dedicated workloads are spiky. 500 requests might take forever.

00:15:44 Rafael Mendes: For spiky workloads, keep them on manual gates. That’s okay for beta.

00:15:51 Lauren Bishop: Agree. Don’t block the feature because some workloads are weird. Just label it.

00:15:58 Marissa Cole: UI can show “insufficient data” and require manual promotion.

00:16:04 Priya Natarajan: Great. Security audit events: Kaitlyn, what must be included?

00:16:10 Kaitlyn Nguyen: Minimum: create_plan, update_plan, start_rollout, promote_stage, abort_rollout, rollback_to_stage, hold, resume, and any force_ action. Payload should include actor_id, actor_type, actor_role, source_ip if available, pool_id, rollout_plan_id, from_stage, to_stage, reason, and a correlation id.

00:16:38 Connor O'Brien: And we need those in export.

00:16:42 Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yes. Otherwise it’s only internal compliance.

00:16:46 Talia Benson: Okay. Now, the 429 incident: is the guardrail work done or pending?

00:16:52 Mina Haddad: Routing change is in; but the validation rule for minimum baseline replicas is pending a final number. Irene and I can sync today.

00:17:04 Irene Choi: We can propose: baseline must be at least 2 replicas per region, canary at least 1 replica, and total must not exceed available by more than one spare node. But we need to map “replica” to actual GPU slices for different model sizes.

00:17:22 Ethan Park: The control plane knows desired “target model build” and resource profile; it can translate to replica sizing. But there are edge cases.

00:17:32 Priya Natarajan: Beta customers should be ones where we control that. We can pick a known model and known runtime build.

00:17:40 Lauren Bishop: Yes. We should not beta on their custom quantization profile day one.

00:17:47 Talia Benson: Alright. We’re at 18 minutes in this transcript, but Fireflies is going to keep going; we don’t need to read every checklist line. Let’s jump to “go-live sequence.” Connor?

00:18:00 Connor O'Brien: Sequence: (1) deploy control plane migration to prod with flag off, (2) deploy routing changes behind flag, (3) deploy telemetry tagging with allowlist—already done, (4) deploy Console UI/Backend, (5) enable for internal dogfood pool, (6) enable for design partner account A, monitor for a week, (7) add partner B.

00:18:28 Rafael Mendes: I want a firm “internal dogfood” step. If we can’t run staged rollout on our own Dedicated pool without pager noise, we’re not ready.

00:18:38 Priya Natarajan: Agreed. That’s basically phase 3 in the plan.

00:18:44 Marissa Cole: Console also needs dogfood feedback. The stage timeline component is new and it’s easy to confuse.

00:18:52 Talia Benson: Great. Anything else that could flip us to “no-go” today?

00:18:58 (crosstalk)

00:19:00 Logan Wright: Only thing is telemetry outages. If Prometheus or whatever is delayed, gate evaluation might block promotion and people will try to force. We need clear guidance: don’t force-promote during telemetry outage unless you have other evidence.

00:19:16 Rafael Mendes: Yeah. Add that to playbook: “If telemetry is degraded, hold. If urgent, abort or rollback, not promote.”

00:19:25 Kaitlyn Nguyen: And if you do override, you must write reason. No blank reasons.

00:19:31 Ethan Park: API enforces non-empty reason for force-promote. For normal promote, it’s optional.

00:19:39 Marissa Cole: Console can require reason only on force actions.

00:19:44 Talia Benson: Perfect. We’ll wrap at 58 minutes in real life, but I’ll keep a bit more transcript for realism.

00:19:52 Connor O'Brien: One more: communications. For beta customers we should provide: runbook excerpt, known limitations (no custom metrics, fail-closed gating, small-pool restrictions), and escalation path.

00:20:08 Lauren Bishop: TAM can do that. Liam’s already prepping the design partner enablement.

00:20:13 Rafael Mendes: Also, alerts: gate failure should create a ticket to owning team, not page on first failure. Page only if the rollout is in progress and customer traffic is impacted.

00:20:26 Allison Grant: Dashboards can annotate promotions. That helps correlate.

00:20:33 Mina Haddad: For streaming requests, rollback semantics: current plan is “in-flight streams are pinned,” we don’t yank them mid-stream. New requests route according to new stage. That means rollback isn’t instantaneous for long streams.

00:20:52 Priya Natarajan: That’s acceptable. We just need to document it and make sure customers understand it.

00:20:57 Marissa Cole: UI can show “Rollback initiated; existing sessions may continue on previous stage for up to N minutes.”

00:21:05 Talia Benson: Okay. I’m going to do a quick recap, then we’ll end.

00:21:10 Talia Benson: Decision is GO for limited beta. Conditions: (1) guardrails for small pools and min headroom, (2) fail-closed on missing metrics with admin-only force-promote/hold, (3) dogfood internally before external enablement, (4) audit export fix required before regulated partner, (5) Console gate evidence and dashboard queries verified post-hotfix.

00:21:35 Talia Benson: Connor sends updated checklist tomorrow. Rafael updates oncall guidance. Logan/Allison on staleness messaging and dashboards verification. Mina/Irene sign off on partitioning validation numbers. Kaitlyn confirms RBAC and audit payload fields.

00:21:54 Talia Benson: Anything I missed?

00:21:56 Priya Natarajan: Sounds right.

00:21:58 Rafael Mendes: Yep.

00:22:00 Connor O'Brien: Good.

00:22:02 Marissa Cole: Good.

00:22:04 Talia Benson: Alright, thanks everyone.

[Call ends]

title: Go/No-Go: Dedicated staged rollouts (beta) + incident review + launch checklist
preview: Internal go/no-go for enabling Dedicated staged rollouts for a limited beta (1–2 design partners). Reviewed early beta failures (429 spike due to canary partition starving effective capacity, gate evidence missing due to telemetry tag mismatch, and metrics cardinality regression). Aligning on ship criteria: feature flag default OFF, validated rollback/abort semantics, stable gate evaluation under telemetry lag, and audit export readiness. Decision: GO for limited beta with guardrails and explicit hold/force-promote controls restricted to admin roles; no broad enablement until SIEM export and small-pool safeguards are verified.
